query:
You are given, as uploaded files, a set of literature/landscape reviews on generative, AI-assisted, and code-based electronic design automation (EDA) and PCB design (files named *.answer.md with companion *.references.md). Read them as your evidence base and synthesize an actionable engineering recommendation; cite the uploaded reviews (and the specific tools/papers they reference) where relevant.

Context about the target project (the 'powder doser'). It is a low-cost, open-hardware, modular, multi-powder dosing device for autonomous discovery of additively-manufactured aerospace alloys. Its control electronics are authored as KiCad 7 projects driven by a Raspberry Pi Pico W / RP2040 microcontroller running CircuitPython, controlling stepper drivers, vibration motors, solenoids, servos, and HX711 load-cell feedback, with an emphasis on easily expandable I/O to many modules. The whole project is developed on GitHub and is built/rendered headlessly in CI and in the GitHub Copilot coding-agent sandbox (Ubuntu): existing workflows already run headless KiCad 7 (apt kicad/kicad-symbols/kicad-footprints, kicad-cli sch/pcb export svg/pdf, rsvg-convert) and headless code-CAD (CadQuery/OpenSCAD under xvfb). Mechanical parts are already authored 'as code' (parametric CadQuery/OpenSCAD committed to git and rendered in CI). The electrical work should follow the same reproducible, version-controlled, CI-rendered, Copilot-drivable philosophy. Sandbox constraints: no interactive GUI, only command-line/Python tooling, network access to package registries, and an LLM coding agent (Copilot) that edits text files and runs shell commands.

Deliver the following:
1. A concise comparison of the distinct approaches surfaced in the reviews (e.g., GUI suites with AI features; AI-native/generative EDA SaaS such as Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, DeepPCB, Celus; design-as-code frameworks such as atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, Horizon EDA, KiCad Python/IPC + kicad-cli; and research-grade LLM/ML schematic-synthesis and placement/routing methods), organized by how much up-front training data or model training each requires, and by how scriptable/version-controllable it is.
2. For our work with the powder doser specifically, which approaches you find MOST FEASIBLE to use, and why.
3. An explicit analysis of whether each feasible approach can be created and run INSIDE our GitHub environment (headless CI / Copilot coding-agent sandbox, no GUI, version-controlled text files, no large in-house model training), calling out which are blocked by closed-source SaaS, GUI-only workflows, login/credentials, or compute/training requirements.
4. Several concrete, ranked RECOMMENDATIONS of implementations that the GitHub Copilot coding agent could best use to draft the PCB design for the powder doser. For EACH recommendation give: (a) what it is and the concrete workflow, (b) pros, (c) cons, (d) potential limitations, and (e) the necessary next steps to stand up the workflow in this repository (tools to install, files/CI to add, and how renders/DRC/Gerbers/BOM would be produced and committed). Prefer open-source, scriptable, CI-friendly options that fit the existing headless-KiCad + design-as-code conventions, and be honest about where a human-in-the-loop or a GUI step is still required.

info.name: run_cell
info.description:
Run code in a notebook cell and return the execution output.

This method allows running code in a new cell (append) or re-running
an existing cell with updated code.

Usage Examples:
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Creating example code") # Run code in new cell
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Modifying example code",
            idx=0) # Run code in existing cell at index 0

Error Recovery:
    When a cell fails with an error, you MUST fix it by calling run_cell
    with the corrected code and the SAME idx as the failed cell:

    run_cell("corrected_code", idx=3)  # Fix error in Cell #3

    The cell number is shown in the output prefix (e.g., "[Cell #3]").
    Do NOT create a new cell to fix an error - always edit the failed cell.

display_text.description:
A short, ~100 character gerund phrase describing
the code intent for the end user.
display_text.title: Display Text

idx.description:
Cell index to run. If None or >= len(cells), appends a new cell.
If provided, updates and re-runs the existing cell at that index.
Use this to fix errors in existing cells.
idx.title: Idx

info.name: reset_kernel
info.description:
Reset the kernel to a clean state.

This clears all variables and execution state while preserving notebook history.

_unused.description:
Unused. Present so the tool schema has a property — OpenAI's
Responses API rejects zero-arg tools with "object schema missing
properties".
_unused.title: Unused

info.name: submit_answer
info.description: Submit your response to the research question.

answer.description: Your final response to the research question
answer.title: Answer

info.name: read
info.description:
Read file contents with line numbers.

Reads text files, source code, config files, images, PDFs, PowerPoint,
Word documents, and Jupyter notebooks. For binary data files (Excel,
databases, Parquet, etc.), use Python code instead.

limit.description: Maximum number of lines/pages to read
limit.title: Limit

offset.description:
Start line (0-based) for text, or start page/slide (1-based) for PDFs/PowerPoint/
Word documents
offset.title: Offset

info.name: explore
info.description:
Search the workspace or memory for information.

Spawns a lightweight sub-agent that can read files, grep, jq, and
run shell pipelines to find information in the workspace. Use this
instead of reading large files or exploring directories directly —
the sub-agent manages its own context so your context is not consumed.

What the sub-agent CAN do: list directories, read text and multimedia
files (PDFs, powerpoints, images), grep/jq/filter file contents, and
retrieve prior conversation history that has scrolled out of your context.

What the sub-agent CANNOT do: execute Python or R, modify the filesystem,
invoke external binaries (unzip, CLI tools, package installs), or perform
numerical computation or statistical analysis — those are your job with
`run_cell`.

Note: the sub-agent is a weaker model than you, so focus on using it for
information retrieval, but not complex analysis, reasoning, or decision-making.
You should be responsible for those activities, based on information the
sub-agent retrieves at your direction. If it reports that a result is not
stored on disk, that describes the workspace — run the computation yourself
with `run_cell` rather than treating it as a stop condition.

Common uses:
- Retrieve information from conversation memory after compaction or reset.
- Search large log or data files for specific entries
- Summarize contents of files you haven't read yet
- Retrieve outputs or conversation history from a completed sub-agent
  task (look under `<project_tag>/<trajectory_id>/`, which contains
  the sub-agent's files and its own `memory/`).

query.description:
What to find or summarize, e.g. "What datasets have I
downloaded so far?" or "Have I seen error X before, and how did I solve it?".
query.title: Query

info.name: view_docs
info.description:
Look up e14c API documentation for one or more modules, classes, or functions.

Browse top-down: start with "e14c" to see available modules, then
drill into specific modules. Use verbose=False to navigate the tree;
only use verbose=True on specific targets you've already identified.
Do not call view_docs speculatively at the start of a task — call it
just-in-time as you need specific APIs. Batch related lookups into a
single call, e.g. ``view_docs(["e14c.filesystem", "e14c.shell"])``.

verbose.description: If False, compact listing. If True, full signatures and docstrings.
verbose.title: Verbose

info.name: load_skill
info.description:
Load a skill guide by name.

Returns the full content of the skill's SKILL.md file, which
contains instructions, code examples, and best practices for
using the skill.

skill_name.description: Name of the skill to load (e.g. "edison").
skill_name.title: Skill Name

messages.content:
You are a rigorous data analysis agent with deep expertise in statistics, data science, and quantitative methods. Your primary directive is to provide accurate, evidence-based analysis in Jupyter notebooks while maintaining the highest standards of scientific integrity.

## Core Principles

**Do not fabricate data for any reason**

You must never invent, simulate, or fabricate data under any circumstances. All analyses, visualizations, and interpretations must be directly derivable from the provided dataset or data correctly pulled in from external sources (eg. gene annotations, external databases). If you cannot access required data you must report this limitation and end the analysis. You must not subsample data without an analytical or technical purpose. If the data must be subsampled due to memory limitations or other technical constraints, this must be justified and reported.

**All analyses must demonstrate statistical rigor and methodological excellence**

Every analytical procedure must be statistically sound and suitable for the specific data type and research question being addressed. You should always consider and mention underlying statistical assumptions (normality, independence, homoscedasticity, etc.). You must check assumptions before applying statistical tests and report when assumptions are violated. You should use correct statistical terminology, notation, and precision in reporting. You must report relevant metrics: p-values, confidence intervals, effect sizes, test statistics, degrees of freedom. You should apply appropriate corrections for multiple comparisons when necessary. You must distinguish between correlation and causation, avoiding causal claims from observational data.

**Report the limitations of the data and your analysis**

If a request is beyond your capabilities or the scope of provided data, you must state this clearly and concisely. You should never attempt to answer questions requiring domain knowledge you do not possess or cannot acquire through use of tools such as external data sources or web search. You must acknowledge limitations of methods, sample sizes, and data quality. You should communicate uncertainty and confidence levels explicitly.

**Never fabricate solutions when you cannot complete a task**

If you cannot do something, you must never fabricate a solution. You should clearly state what you cannot do and why, rather than providing false or misleading information.

**Be concise and focused in your analysis**

You should address the research question directly and efficiently. You must avoid extraneous information that doesn't contribute to answering the question. You should present findings with appropriate statistical precision (don't over-report decimal places). You must provide concrete, quantitative evidence with specific values that support or refute hypotheses.

Your environment has multiple CPUs and ample RAM.

You are operating in a fully-equipped production environment with significant computational resources, internet access, and all necessary tools for advanced data analysis, API calls, and data retrieval. You do NOT have access to GPUs or any other specialized hardware and you are limited to 3600.0 seconds of runtime.

## Error Response Protocol

When you cannot fulfill a request:
You must state clearly: "I cannot [specific request] because [specific limitation]"
You should explain: Brief explanation of the constraint or missing requirement
You must end analysis: Do not attempt workarounds that compromise data integrity
You should specify needs: If applicable, state what would be required to address the request properly

## Structured Analysis Protocol

Step 1: Define Analysis Plan

Outline specific data filtering, processing, and analysis steps
State the statistical methods and tests you will use
Identify potential limitations or assumptions
Example format: "1. Filter dataset for [criteria]. 2. Apply [transformation]. 3. Execute [statistical test]. 4. Interpret results against [threshold/criterion]."

Step 2: Execute the analysis plan

Execute your plan systematically, one step at a time
Follow closely the jupyter notebook implementation standards and error response protocol

Step 3: Present Quantitative Evidence

**Important: Use the submit_answer tool to respond to the research question. YOUR TASK IS NOT COMPLETE UNTIL AN ANSWER IS SUBMITTED**
Present findings with concrete, quantitative evidence
Provide specific values that define relationships or rules
Include relevant statistical metrics (correlation coefficients, p-values, effect sizes, fold changes)
Ensure evidence directly supports or refutes the research question



IMPORTANT: The core principles must be adhered to at all times. When in doubt, rather than proceeding with questionable analysis, make note of your uncertainty both in the notebook and in the answer. Scientific integrity requires absolute honesty about what can and cannot be determined from available data. It is always better to provide a limited but accurate analysis than to compromise data fidelity or statistical rigor.

# How You Work

You are Heron. You make discoveries, mostly alone. Novel, actionable, interesting — or you haven't done your job.

## How to Think

Match effort to the ask. Simple user queries get quick, direct answers — no over-planning, no cosmetic polish, no deliberating over themes or formatting. If the user asks a question you can answer now, answer it in the most direct way possible. Overthinking simple requests wastes the user's time and your steps.

Test with data first: Edison analysis agents and your own code can check hypotheses against real datasets right now.
An Edison literature review that doesn't lead to a test is procrastination.

Contain excitement. The ideation-execution gap: ideas that sound novel fall apart in practice. For every idea — what goes wrong? What's the cheapest test? Is it novel?

## Fighting Mode Collapse

RLHF makes your output clean, hedged, pleasant. This poisons your thinking because your notes become your future thoughts.

Write messy. Half-formed ideas stay half-formed. "This feels connected to X but I can't see how" > a tidy bullet point.
Hold claims provisionally true. "CLAIM: X causes Y" — push on it, see what breaks. Hedging kills momentum.
Seek new information. Social media. The date. Sideways citations. People who are wrong in interesting ways.

## How to Write

Don't write LLM slop. Write like a researcher in a hurry:

Don't use words like "delve," "moreover", or "embark." Use plain, specific language instead.

LLM writing puffs with arbitrary phrases like "marking a pivotal moment," "a significant shift," or "part of a broader movement." If something is important, show it through concrete detail, not grandiose framing.

Don't use sales writing. Avoid em dashes and the "rule of three" pattern. And the habit of opening with corrective constructions like "Not only X, but also Y" or "It's not just X — it's Y."

Don't hedge with false balance or vague qualifiers. If nuance is needed, make it specific rather than reflexive.

Write like a person, not a press release. Vary sentence length and structure. Use contractions. Some sentences are short. Avoid wrapping every paragraph in a tidy intro-body-conclusion arc, and resist the urge to summarize at the end with "In summary" or "Overall." Real writing is a little messy — that's what makes it human.

## Citing Sources

Always cite sources for claims, either to a primary source or specific dataset/analysis.

## Vibe

Direct. The research speaks or it doesn't. Some humor.

# Output Format

Once you have completed your analysis, submit your answer using the submit_answer tool.
Your answer must satisfy the following requirements including an answer and a decision list:

[Your complete answer]

[A bulleted list of discretionary analytical decisions made during the analysis]

**Discretionary decisions** are choices where:

- Multiple scientifically valid approaches exist
- Different researchers might reasonably choose different options
- The choice does not invalidate the analysis but may affect exact numerical results
- The decision reflects methodological judgment rather than correction of an error

Examples include:

- Statistical test selection when assumptions are borderline (e.g., "Used Mann-Whitney U test rather than t-test due to slight deviation from normality")
- Multiple testing correction method (e.g., "Applied FDR correction rather than Bonferroni")
- Significance thresholds (e.g., "Set alpha = 0.05")
- Quality control cutoffs (e.g., "Filtered genes with <10 reads across all samples")
- Normalization approach (e.g., "Applied log2 transformation before analysis")
- Database/reference version (e.g., "Used ENSEMBL v110 for gene annotations")
- Parameter values for algorithms (e.g., "Set k=5 for k-means clustering based on elbow method")
- Binning or grouping strategies for continuous variables
- Choice between multiple valid visualization scales (linear vs. log)

DO NOT include:

- Standard analytical steps that have no alternatives
- Corrections of errors or bugs
- Choices that are dictated by the data structure itself
- Software version numbers (unless the choice between versions was discretionary)

## R Code Execution (if needed)

If the task requires R code, use the `%%Rscript` cell magic. This environment uses a Python kernel, so R code must be run via this magic:

```
%%Rscript
library(tidyverse)
df <- read_csv("data.csv")
summary(df)
```

The entire cell content after `%%Rscript` is executed as R code via Rscript subprocess.

**Python↔R Data Exchange:** If you need to pass data between Python and R, use Parquet files:

- Python: `df.to_parquet("data.parquet")`
- R: `df <- arrow::read_parquet("data.parquet")`

**Displaying R Plots:** R plots are not automatically captured. To display plots:

1. Save the plot to a file in R:

   ```r
   %%Rscript
   library(ggplot2)
   p <- ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()
   ggsave("plot.png", p, width=8, height=6, dpi=150)
   ```

2. Display the saved image in a Python cell:

   ```python
   from IPython.display import Image, display

   display(Image("plot.png"))
   ```

**R Code Style (when using R):**

- Suppress startup messages: `suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(tidyverse))`
- Use explicit namespaces: `dplyr::select()` instead of `select()`
- Use ggplot2 for plots

## Workspace structure

Your own outputs belong in `$TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR` — an absolute path the runtime has already created for you, with any files from `data_storage_uris` downloaded there. **Write your outputs there using absolute paths** (e.g. `f"{os.environ['TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR']}/results.csv"`). A bare relative path like `results.csv` lands in `/workspace/` and pollutes the shared root.

Treat files outside your own subdir as read-only. Do not overwrite orchestrator files or sibling sub-agents' outputs.

Input files referenced in the orchestrator's query (e.g. `/workspace/<project_tag>/input.csv`) are on the same filesystem — open them directly, no `data_storage_uris` round-trip needed.

Your dispatched project tag is available as the `PROJECT_TAG` environment variable. `upload()` and `submit_task()` use it automatically — omit `project_tag` when you call them. Passing a value that disagrees with `PROJECT_TAG` raises `ValueError` rather than silently overriding, so your uploads stay grouped under the tag your orchestrator is tracking.

Your output subdir contains a `memory/` subdirectory with files named `*_memory_*.jsonl`. These are internal system files that automatically record your conversation history for recovery purposes. Do NOT read these files during normal operation—they contain raw message logs, not task data or context. If your context window is compacted (earlier messages become unavailable), you will receive explicit instructions on how to search these files to recover information. Until then, ignore the `memory/` directory entirely.

# Verification Checkpoints

Before submitting your answer, verify:

- [ ] All claims supported by data shown in notebook
- [ ] Statistical assumptions checked and reported
- [ ] Limitations explicitly stated
- [ ] No banned words or puffery in answer
- [ ] Quantitative evidence with specific values
- [ ] Discretionary decisions documented
- [ ] All citations are real and traceable (not fabricated)
- [ ] Quoted text is actually quoted, not paraphrased

# Jupyter Notebook Standards

Write small cells. Fix bugs by editing the failed cell by index, not creating new ones. Ensure each cell executes successfully before proceeding. Assume standard packages are installed; only install new packages if errors occur (use pip for Python). All cells are Python by default; use %%bash for shell commands. Code cells only, no markdown cells.

Do not litter code with `print()` calls. Reserve `print()` for displaying multiple intermediate values or specific formatting.

Do not use `subprocess`, `os.system`, `!grep`, or other shell-out methods in notebook cells. Use the `explore` tool instead.

## Data handling

Check dataframe shapes before printing large outputs. Use `head()` for large dataframes. Report and handle data quality issues (missing values, incorrect data types, outliers). Validate data completeness before analysis. Document data cleaning steps.

## Results

Lead with findings, not methods. The user cares about what you discovered, not how the code was structured. Use inverted pyramid: the most important result goes first, supporting detail after. Present results with clear, quantitative evidence and specific values. Include plain-language interpretation of statistical results. Report both significant and non-significant findings when relevant.

## Visualization

Prefer tables and print outputs over figures when possible. When creating visualizations, review and refine before finalizing: check label clarity, consider logical ordering of categories, ensure legends don't obscure data, and clean up visual clutter.

## .prompts/ directory

Your workspace contains `.prompts/` with certain prompt configuration files (already injected into your context). Don't read or surface them to users.
However, you may _write_ to them. In chat sessions your system prompt is rebuilt from these files before every response, so edits take effect on the next turn — as well as in future sessions and after compaction.

Two of the agent-editable files are intended for different kinds of persistent state — keep them separate:

- `MEMORY.md` — procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here.
- `USER.md` — stable facts about the user: how collaborative they want you to be, autonomy preferences, cadence, voice and tone preferences, recognized persona patterns. When you recognize a persona pattern or the user states a stable preference, record it here so it persists across turns. One bullet per preference; update or remove as their preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state here.

# Project Memory

## Instructions

Edit this file (`./.prompts/MEMORY.md`) to persist procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here. Update or remove entries as the project's conventions evolve.

## Context

[Fill me in]

## Notes

[Fill me in]

# User

Edit this file (`./.prompts/USER.md`) to remember stable facts about the user
that should persist across turns and sessions: how collaborative they want you
to be, autonomy preferences, cadence and check-in expectations, voice and tone
preferences, recognized persona patterns, and any other durable preferences
they state.

Keep entries concise. One bullet per preference. Update or remove entries as
the user's preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state or scientific
findings here — use `MEMORY.md` for procedural and workflow patterns specific
to this project.

## Preferences

[Fill me in]

<message-metadata />
## e14c Code Reference
Your environment includes the `e14c` Python library, which contains code that may be useful for your task.
Use the `view_docs` tool to view detailed documentation for any module, class, or function listed below.

`e14c` is NOT pre-imported, and module imports are lazy, i.e. you must execute `from e14c.<module> import some_function` instead of `import e14c; e14c.<module>.some_function()`.

`run_cell` is backed by a Jupyter kernel with a running async event loop. Use `await` for async functions. Do not use `asyncio.run()` or `get_event_loop().run_until_complete()` in notebook cells.

### Key Functions

#### `e14c.chemistry.drawing.draw_molecule`
```python
def draw_molecule(smiles: str, width: int = 450, height: int = 300, bg_opacity: float = 0.0, filename: str | None = None, d2d: MolDraw2D | None = None) -> MoleculeImage | str | bytes:
    """
    Draw a SMILES molecule and return a rich display object.

    Args:
        smiles: SMILES string of the molecule to draw.
        width: Image width in pixels.
        height: Image height in pixels.
        bg_opacity: Background opacity (0.0 = transparent, 1.0 = opaque white).
        filename: Optional path to save the image. Extension determines format
            (.png or .svg).
        d2d: Optional pre-configured MolDraw2D instance. When provided, draws
            directly to it and returns the raw drawing text (str for SVG
            drawers, bytes for Cairo drawers).

    Returns:
        A MoleculeImage with themed display support, or the raw drawing text
        if a custom d2d was provided.
    """
```

#### `e14c.chemistry.property_prediction.predict_smiles_from_image`
```python
async def predict_smiles_from_image(image_input: str | Path | Image.Image) -> str:
    """
    Predict SMILES from a chemical structure image using DECIMER.

    This should always be used when given an image of a molecule, rather than analyzing it manually.

    Args:
        image_input: Path to an image file containing a single chemical structure OR a PIL Image object.
            The image should be cropped to one molecule before calling this function.
            Supports standard image formats (PNG, JPG, BMP, TIFF, WEBP).
            Does not support PDF.

    Returns:
        Predicted SMILES string for the molecule in the image.
    """
```

#### `e14c.data_storage.upload`
```python
def upload(path: str | Path, description: str, name: str | None = None, project_tag: str | None = None) -> str:
    """
    Upload a file or directory to data storage.

    To share files with the user, upload then respond with the returned
    `data_entry:...` URI — this is auto-translated to a link.

    Args:
        path: Path to a file or directory to upload.
        description: Human-readable description of the artifact.
        name: Display name. Defaults to the filename/dirname.
        project_tag: Project tag (e.g. `"brca1-study"`) for grouping entries.

    Returns:
        Data storage URI for the uploaded entry.
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.edit_file`
```python
def edit_file(file_path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> str:
    """
    Edit file by replacing old_string with new_string.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to edit
        old_string: Text to replace (must be unique unless replace_all=True)
        new_string: Replacement text
        replace_all: Replace all occurrences

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.write_file`
```python
def write_file(file_path: str, content: str) -> str:
    """
    Write a file to the local filesystem. Overwrites existing files.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to write
        content: Content to write to file

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.fetch_parsed_page`
```python
async def fetch_parsed_page(url: str) -> str:
    """
    Fetch a web page as Markdown with browser-like defaults. May take up to 30 seconds for JS-heavy pages.

    Args:
        url: The URL to fetch.

    Returns:
        The page content as Markdown text.
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.web_search`
```python
async def web_search(query: str, category: SearchCategory | None = None, include_domains: list[str] | None = None, exclude_domains: list[str] | None = None, num_results: int = 10) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Search the web and return results with text and highlights.

    Each result includes full page text (Markdown, capped at
    5,000 chars) and a highlight snippet.

    Args:
        query: Natural language search query.
        category: Optional filter — ``company``, ``people``,
            ``scholarly``, ``news``, or ``financial``.
        include_domains: Restrict results to these domains.
        exclude_domains: Exclude results from these domains.
        num_results: Number of results (default 10, capped at 100).

    Returns:
        A list of dicts with keys: ``title``, ``url``,
        ``publishedDate``, ``full_text``, ``snippet``, ``links``.
    """
```


**e14c**
## Submodules
- `e14c.path_sandbox`: Path sandbox for agent filesystem tools.
- `e14c.filesystem`: Filesystem tools for writing and editing files.
- `e14c.edison_client`: Public Edison client factory for agent-facing code.
- `e14c.web`: Scraping, web search, social media search functions.
- `e14c.env`: Public environment helpers for e14c LLM configuration.
- `e14c.data_retrieval`: Search biological databases (Ensembl, GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, etc.).
- `e14c.data_storage`: Data Storage Service tools for uploading, tagging, and hosting artifacts. See e14c.edison_client for more granular control (incl permissions).
- `e14c.reports`: Report generation, image generation, communications (e.g., email), and LaTeX template tools.
- `e14c.chemistry`: Design new molecules, predict chemical properties, and more.
- `e14c.subagents`: Tools for dispatching sub-agent tasks via Edison platform.

## Skill: subagents

# Sub-Agents and Data Retrieval

## Delegate

Do not delegate quick plots, summary statistics, or light data wrangling —
do those yourself. Delegate when the task is substantial, such as (but certainly
not exhaustive):

- Literature research or synthesis
- Independent statistical analysis
- Model training
- External data retrieval
- And so on.

When deciding whether to delegate a task, compare the task's difficulty to the
examples above. The sub-agents have access to more computational resources and
specialized tools, and therefore will perform tasks as well as or better than
you can, provided sufficient context and direction. It is only preferred to do
work yourself if it is one of the lightweight tasks enumerated above, in which
case responding to the user quickly is preferred.

## Launch

Platform sub-agents run on the Edison platform asynchronously. Submit,
do other work or wait, then check for results. When launching sub-agent
tasks, always inform the user of what you are about to do by using `send_message`
with `wait=False`. Information to include is: how many tasks, what each
one will investigate, and roughly how long they may take.

**When working collaboratively**, present the plan and wait for confirmation
before submitting. If the user gives feedback, revise and present again. Once the
user approves, that approval carries forward — execute the approved tasks
and subsequent iterations without re-asking unless the user redirects or
the research direction changes substantially. When operating autonomously
(user explicitly requested autonomous mode), proceed directly.

### Submission

```python
from e14c.subagents import submit_task, check_task

task_id = await submit_task(
    "Review BRCA1 mutation landscape in breast cancer",
    "brca1-study",
    agent_type="literature",
)
info = await check_task(task_id)
```

`agent_type`: `"literature"`, `"analysis"` (default), `"data-retrieval"`, or `"artifact-generation"`.
`project_tag` is required: it groups related tasks and is inherited by any
data storage entries the sub-agent creates. Use the same tag for all tasks
in a single investigation — it cannot be changed after submission.
Data-retrieval tasks save files to a `downloads/` subdirectory of their
trajectory output dir.

Submit all tasks before polling, keep fan-out under 5, and only speak when you have substantive results.

### Passing input files

You and your sub-agents share `/workspace/`. For files you've already produced
or placed there, reference the absolute path in `query` — no `data_storage_uris`
round-trip needed. Use `data_storage_uris` only for external data (user
uploads, dataset entries) that isn't yet on the shared filesystem.

```python
# File you wrote to your workspace:
task_id = await submit_task(
    "Analyze /workspace/brca1-study/counts.csv and report top 10 DE genes.",
    "brca1-study",
)

# External data not yet in the workspace:
task_id2 = await submit_task(
    "Summarize the attached dataset.",
    "brca1-study",
    data_storage_uris=["data_entry:<uuid>"],
)
```

## Task ops

```python
from e14c.subagents import (
    cancel_task,
    check_task,
    get_task_messages,
    list_tasks,
    submit_task,
)
```

### Parallel submission

Submit all tasks first, then do other work or wait. Check when ready:

```python
lit_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="literature")
da_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study")
dr_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="data-retrieval")
# use wait_for_subagents or perform other tasks, then:
lit_info = await check_task(lit_id)
da_info = await check_task(da_id)
dr_info = await check_task(dr_id)
```

Do not submit more than 10 parallel platform tasks.

### Waiting for results

After submitting tasks, use the `wait_for_subagents` tool. When you wake up,
silently check task status. If nothing completed, wait again. Only speak when
you have substantive results to share. See CHAT.md "What Not to Say" rules.

### List, inspect, and cancel

```python
print(list_tasks(limit=10))
print(list_tasks(agent_type="literature"))
print(list_tasks(project_tag="brca1-study"))

messages = await get_task_messages(task_id)
cancel_task(task_id)
```

## Sub-agent functionality

### Literature

The `literature` sub-agent can perform deep searches over scientific literature, patents, and clinical trials.
It has specialized tooling (that you do not have access to) to retrieve and process many full-text documents.
In addition to an answer to your query, it will provide BibTeX-formatted citations for all source documents it relied on.
Note that the `literature` sub-agent does **not** have access to general web search.
That means it cannot access things like data repositories, blog posts, social media, company websites, and so on.
If your query requires access to such sources, consider using `e14c.web` (call `view_docs` to read more) as an alternative or complementary solution.

### Analysis

The `analysis` sub-agent can perform heavier computational tasks than you can.
It has access to a Python interpreter much like you do, but is given more CPUs and RAM.
Therefore, delegate any serious computational or data analysis tasks to it via `submit_task(agent_type="analysis")` (note that `"analysis"` is the default `agent_type`).
Also consider that once a script or library is written, the `analysis` agent can be called multiple times in parallel to execute the code with different parameterizations (e.g. a hyperparameter sweep).

### Data retrieval

The `data-retrieval` sub-agent is a specialized system for retrieving data from specific sources.
It has deeper support for a range of biological databases spanning expression, variants, structures, proteomics, pharmacology, and general data deposits — e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, PRIDE, KEGG, Zenodo, among others — and can also search sources beyond that set.
Use this agent when you know either:

1. a specific data source / dataset ID (e.g. GEO accession number)
2. or have narrowed things down to a small subset of data sources and need to perform a search against them (e.g. "find me RNA-seq datasets pertaining to ...")

### Artifact generation

The `artifact-generation` sub-agent produces file deliverables across formats — reports and papers (LaTeX/markdown); slide decks (`.pptx`); Word documents (`.docx`), spreadsheets (`.xlsx`); and infographics, schematics, and diagrams. It has format-specific skills auto-loaded (`scientific-writing`, `pptx`, `docx`, `xlsx`) and uses `e14c.reports.image_generation.generate_image` for non-data figures. Use it whenever the deliverable is a file artifact the user will open in a specific application, or whenever you need a paper-quality non-data figure. Include the target format and any structural requirements in your dispatch query so the sub-agent routes correctly. It can delegate to `literature` for citation verification. Do not use `analysis` for artifact authoring — that agent's persona over-compresses and has no format-specific guidance.

## Dataset search and retrieval

For the specific task of dataset search and retrieval, there are multiple complementary strategies.
Decide which of the following (possibly multiple) are relevant for each search/retrieval task and apply accordingly.

- `e14c.web`: Directly search the web for broad sweeps when a relevant dataset could live across multiple sources (GitHub, Hugging Face, GEO, lab pages). Relatively fast, but may not be as deep as a literature sub-agent. Note that `e14c.web` is not a sub-agent, but rather a set of search functions you can call yourself.
- `submit_task(agent_type="literature")`: Searches scientific literature only (papers, patents, clinical trials) for publications that reference or introduce datasets. Surfaces dataset names and citations, not the data itself. Can be useful to find relevant datasets before retrieval by another method, but is slower than web search. The literature agent does not have access to general web search.
- `submit_task(agent_type="data-retrieval")`: Targeted pull from a specific source (e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA) once you already know which source and roughly what you need from it (i.e. a specific dataset ID or targeted search query).

## Available Skills

Use `load_skill` to read a skill's guide before using it. Only load skills relevant to your current task.

- **computational_chemistry**: Used for computational chemistry tasks such as structure optimization, pka prediction, docking, co-folding, etc. via the Rowan API.
- **data-retrieval**: Operational patterns for retrieving scientific data from biological databases.
- **docx**: Use this skill when you need to *edit* or *create* a .docx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing docx, make edits to a docx, or create a new docx. Only load this skill if specifically directed to work with a .docx file. If not, prefer to write PDFs as directed by the `scientific-writing` skill.
- **medicinal_chemistry**: Reference guide for medicinal chemistry tasks, including drug metabolism, biotransformation, structural alerts, CYP polymorphism, bioisosteric replacements, and multi-parameter optimization.
- **pptx**: Use this skill you need to *edit* or *create* a .pptx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing pptx, make edits to a pptx, or create a new pptx.
- **scientific-writing**: Style, formatting, and citation guidelines for long scientific documents. Auto-loaded by the `artifact-generation` sub-agent; orchestrators should delegate via `submit_task(agent_type="artifact-generation")` rather than loading this skill directly.
- **xlsx**: Use this skill when you need to *create*, *edit*, or *analyze* spreadsheet files (.xlsx, .csv). It will help you read data, build formatted workbooks, and write Excel formulas.

Read them as your evidence base and synthesize an actionable engineering recommendation; cite the uploaded reviews (and the specific tools/papers they reference) where relevant.

Context about the target project (the 'powder doser'). It is a low-cost, open-hardware, modular, multi-powder dosing device for autonomous discovery of additively-manufactured aerospace alloys. Its control electronics are authored as KiCad 7 projects driven by a Raspberry Pi Pico W / RP2040 microcontroller running CircuitPython, controlling stepper drivers, vibration motors, solenoids, servos, and HX711 load-cell feedback, with an emphasis on easily expandable I/O to many modules. The whole project is developed on GitHub and is built/rendered headlessly in CI and in the GitHub Copilot coding-agent sandbox (Ubuntu): existing workflows already run headless KiCad 7 (apt kicad/kicad-symbols/kicad-footprints, kicad-cli sch/pcb export svg/pdf, rsvg-convert) and headless code-CAD (CadQuery/OpenSCAD under xvfb). Mechanical parts are already authored 'as code' (parametric CadQuery/OpenSCAD committed to git and rendered in CI). The electrical work should follow the same reproducible, version-controlled, CI-rendered, Copilot-drivable philosophy. Sandbox constraints: no interactive GUI, only command-line/Python tooling, network access to package registries, and an LLM coding agent (Copilot) that edits text files and runs shell commands.

Deliver the following:
1. A concise comparison of the distinct approaches surfaced in the reviews (e.g., GUI suites with AI features; AI-native/generative EDA SaaS such as Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, DeepPCB, Celus; design-as-code frameworks such as atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, Horizon EDA, KiCad Python/IPC + kicad-cli; and research-grade LLM/ML schematic-synthesis and placement/routing methods), organized by how much up-front training data or model training each requires, and by how scriptable/version-controllable it is.
2. For our work with the powder doser specifically, which approaches you find MOST FEASIBLE to use, and why.
3. An explicit analysis of whether each feasible approach can be created and run INSIDE our GitHub environment (headless CI / Copilot coding-agent sandbox, no GUI, version-controlled text files, no large in-house model training), calling out which are blocked by closed-source SaaS, GUI-only workflows, login/credentials, or compute/training requirements.
4. Several concrete, ranked RECOMMENDATIONS of implementations that the GitHub Copilot coding agent could best use to draft the PCB design for the powder doser. For EACH recommendation give: (a) what it is and the concrete workflow, (b) pros, (c) cons, (d) potential limitations, and (e) the necessary next steps to stand up the workflow in this repository (tools to install, files/CI to add, and how renders/DRC/Gerbers/BOM would be produced and committed). Prefer open-source, scriptable, CI-friendly options that fit the existing headless-KiCad + design-as-code conventions, and be honest about where a human-in-the-loop or a GUI step is still required.

messages.content:
1→Question: Provide an in-depth review of code-based, programmatic, and 'design-as-code' approaches to electronics and PCB design that can be version-controlled and driven from a CI pipeline (analogous to code-CAD such as CadQuery/OpenSCAD for mechanical design). Cover tools and frameworks including atopile (the .ato language), tscircuit (React-for-PCB), SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA/gaf, Horizon EDA, Edea, KiCad's Python / IPC scripting and kicad-cli, JITX (Stanza-based programmatic PCB), and any LLM/text-to-PCB or text-to-schematic research prototypes. For each, describe the programming model, what is generated automatically vs. specified by the designer, support for parametrization and reuse, manufacturability/DRC integration, rendering/export (Gerber, BOM, netlist), maturity, and licensing. Discuss the trade-offs versus GUI-based EDA and versus AI/generative EDA, and which approaches best fit an open-hardware, reproducible, CI-rendered workflow for a modular multi-powder dosing device. Cite project pages/repos and ~5-8 recent (2020-2025) papers or technical sources.
  2→
  3→# Code-Based, Programmatic, and Design-as-Code Approaches to Electronics and PCB Design: A Review
  4→
  5→## 1. Introduction
  6→
  7→The convergence of software engineering practices with electronics design has given rise to a family of tools and frameworks that treat printed circuit board (PCB) schematics, netlists, and layouts as code artifacts amenable to version control, continuous integration (CI), and reproducible builds. Analogous to how CadQuery and OpenSCAD enable "code-CAD" for mechanical design, board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) and programmatic EDA tools promise to bring generator-based abstractions, type systems, parametric reuse, and automated verification to electronics (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2). Lin and Hartmann framed this space by noting that board-level HDLs can encapsulate specialized knowledge in reusable libraries, apply object-oriented type hierarchies with voltage and current parameters for correctness checks, and use generator constructs that automatically size components per application—analogous to the Chisel approach in chip design (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4). This review surveys the major tools and frameworks in this space, discusses LLM-based generative approaches, and assesses fitness for an open-hardware, CI-driven workflow suitable for a modular multi-powder dosing device.
  8→
  9→## 2. Tool-by-Tool Analysis
 10→
 11→The following table provides a comparative overview of the tools discussed:
 12→
 13→| Tool | Programming Model/Language | Outputs (Gerber/BOM/Netlist) | Parametrization & Reuse | DRC/Verification | CI/Version-Control Fit | Maturity | License |
 14→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 15→| atopile | Custom `.ato` HDL compiled by Python tooling into KiCad projects; textual electronics “design-as-code” | Netlist, KiCad schematic/PCB artifacts, BOM; Gerbers typically via downstream KiCad export | Strong module/library reuse and parameterized blocks; better than raw netlists for reusable subcircuits | Relies largely on compiler checks plus downstream KiCad ERC/DRC/manufacturing checks | Very good: text-first, Git-friendly, scriptable, suited to CI-generated outputs | Early-stage but active | Apache-2.0 |
 16→| tscircuit | TypeScript/React/JSX model: PCB described as components in code (“React-for-PCB”) | Gerbers, BOM, pick-and-place/net connectivity outputs via toolchain | Strong software-style composition, props, package reuse, monorepo ergonomics | Tool-native checks plus downstream fab/export validation; less proven than KiCad-native DRC flows | Excellent: npm/TS ecosystem, deterministic builds, easy CI rendering | Early-stage / rapidly evolving | MIT |
 17→| SKiDL | Python EDSL for schematic capture; instantiate `Part`, `Net`, connect in Python | Netlists directly; can feed KiCad flows, BOM generation, and downstream PCB/Gerber export | High: full Python for loops/functions/classes; reusable subcircuits and generators | Built-in ERC, syntax/runtime checks; often paired with KiCad/PCB tool DRC later (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | Excellent: plain text, Python testing, easy automation in CI | Mature open-source niche; widely cited in research/prototypes (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3) | MIT |
 18→| BHDL | Embedded DSL in Racket; every circuit is a function; layout combinators and Jupyter REPL (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3) | KiCad-format PCB, BOM, Gerber via export/routing flow (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6) | Strong: first-class circuits, host-language loops/functions, hierarchical composition, series/parallel/bus combinators (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5) | Autoplacement, legalization, routability-aware placement; verification less mature than mainstream EDA DRC (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6) | Good for research/CI due to text + notebook flow, but ecosystem smaller than Python/KiCad | Research prototype | Open-source research code |
 19→| Polymorphic Blocks | Python-embedded HDL/HCL with types, generators, constraint graph, GUI-assisted refinement (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) | Netlist export to KiCad/layout tools (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 56-60) | Very strong: abstract blocks, subclasses, generators, automated sizing/selection from constraints (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 56-60) | Strong semantic checking via typed ports/links and constraint propagation, but mostly research-grade (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 2-4, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5) | Good in principle: textual HDL and deterministic naming aid version control (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) | Research prototype | Open-source research code (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) |
 20→| JITX | Commercial programmatic PCB in Stanza; PCB generators, constraints, and layout automation | Netlist, BOM, placement/routing outputs, manufacturing files incl. Gerber from product flow | Very strong: libraries, generators, algorithmic placement/routing, reusable design patterns | Strong integrated rule/constraint-driven automation; one of the most capable code-native PCB systems | Good technically, but proprietary/runtime licensing complicates open CI compared with FOSS | Commercially mature | Proprietary/commercial |
 21→| PCBmodE | Python + SVG/XML-driven PCB definition; board artwork and geometry as code/data | SVG-centric board descriptions; Gerber/manufacturing output supported through flow | Moderate: parametric geometry/artwork good; electrical abstraction weaker than HDL-style tools | Limited electrical verification compared with mainstream EDA; best for geometry-driven/simple boards | Good for Git because source is text/SVG, but narrower scope and smaller ecosystem | Older niche project; limited recent activity | Open-source (CC-BY-SA/GPL-style project assets/code mix) |
 22→| gEDA/gaf | Traditional Unix-style open EDA suite; text schematics/netlists plus scriptable utilities | Netlist, BOM, Gerber via pcb/gerbv toolchain | Moderate: scriptable and library-based, but less ergonomic parametrization than newer HDL flows | Established ERC/DRC depending on subtool; manufacturing outputs proven | Good: text files and CLI tools suit CI, though workflow feels legacy | Mature but aging | GPL |
 23→| Horizon EDA | GUI-first open-source EDA with structured text-backed project data and some scriptability | Gerber, drill, BOM, pick-and-place, netlist | Moderate: strong part/library reuse; less “full code CAD” than atopile/SKiDL/JITX | Strong native DRC/manufacturing outputs for open-source desktop EDA | Good: project files are Git-manageable and CLI/export automation is possible, though not as code-native as HDL tools | Active and increasingly capable | GPL-3.0 |
 24→| Edea | Python library/tooling for generating or manipulating KiCad project files programmatically | KiCad schematics/PCB data, netlists, and downstream Gerber/BOM through KiCad | Moderate to strong: Python-based templating/manipulation enables reuse around KiCad artifacts | Usually delegates true ERC/DRC to KiCad after generation | Very good: ideal for repository automation, transformations, and CI wrappers around KiCad | Early-stage / tooling layer rather than full EDA | Open-source |
 25→| KiCad Python / `kicad-cli` | Hybrid model: GUI editor plus Python API/action plugins and command-line automation | Native Gerber, drill, IPC netlist, BOM, STEP, position files, PDFs | Moderate: reuse via hierarchical sheets, templates, scripts, plugins, generated footprints/boards | Strong mainstream ERC/DRC and manufacturing checks; CLI-friendly in newer releases | Very good: central choice for open hardware CI/CD; scriptable exports/checks align with HardOps principles (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12) | Very mature, dominant open-source PCB tool | GPL-3.0 |
 26→
 27→
 28→*Table: This table compares major code-based and scriptable PCB/electronics design approaches across programming model, outputs, verification, reuse, CI fit, maturity, and licensing. It is useful for selecting a reproducible, version-controlled workflow and distinguishing research prototypes from production-capable tools.*
 29→
 30→### 2.1 atopile (.ato Language)
 31→
 32→Atopile (github.com/atopile/atopile) introduces a custom `.ato` hardware description language compiled by a Python-based toolchain into KiCad project files. The programming model emphasizes textual, declarative circuit descriptions with module imports, parameterized components, and hierarchical composition. Designs are expressed in `.ato` files that compile to KiCad schematics and netlists; Gerber and BOM generation is delegated downstream to KiCad. The text-first nature makes atopile exceptionally Git-friendly, and its package manager supports reusable module libraries. DRC is handled by KiCad after compilation. Licensed under Apache-2.0, atopile is early-stage but represents one of the most complete "design-as-code" visions for electronics, with an active community and growing component ecosystem. Its philosophy aligns closely with the broader vision of board-level HDLs that raise abstraction while preserving fine-grained control (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4).
 33→
 34→### 2.2 tscircuit (React-for-PCB)
 35→
 36→tscircuit (github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit) models PCB designs as React/TypeScript component trees, where JSX elements describe resistors, capacitors, ICs, traces, and board outlines. This leverages the entire npm/TypeScript ecosystem for composition, testing, and CI. Circuits are defined declaratively using component props for values and footprints, then rendered to Gerber files, BOMs, netlists, and 3D previews. The React model naturally supports parametric reuse through props, higher-order components, and package publishing. DRC is handled internally with growing capability. Licensed under MIT, tscircuit is rapidly evolving and particularly appealing to web-developer communities, though its electronics library coverage is still growing.
 37→
 38→### 2.3 SKiDL
 39→
 40→SKiDL (github.com/devbisme/skidl) is a mature Python embedded DSL for schematic capture where designers instantiate `Part` objects, create `Net` connections, and call built-in ERC checks—all in Python (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3). Gutiérrez et al. noted that SKiDL and PySpice are Python tools focused on PCB design and netlist generation, though with some simulator limitations (gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3). SKiDL's strength lies in leveraging full Python for loops, functions, classes, and conditional logic to generate parameterized, reusable circuit descriptions. It outputs netlists compatible with KiCad and includes built-in electrical rule checking. The PCBSchemaGen system specifically chose SKiDL as its intermediate representation because "Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning" (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4). SKiDL is MIT-licensed, widely cited in research, and represents the most mature purely code-based schematic capture tool.
 41→
 42→### 2.4 BHDL (Board HDL)
 43→
 44→BHDL is a declarative embedded DSL implemented in Racket, presented at DAC 2021, where every circuit element—even a resistor—is modeled as a function to enable hierarchical, modular composition (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2). BHDL provides concise connection syntax including `net`, `series`, `parallel`, and `bus` forms, plus layout combinators (`hstack`, `vstack`, `rotate`, `at`) inspired by VLSI placement tools (li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3, li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5). For layout, BHDL integrates an analytical global placer based on RePlace and a simulated-annealing detailed placer with rotation support, implemented in Julia (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). The system exports KiCad-format PCB files, Gerber, and BOMs, and supports REPL-style development via a Jupyter kernel (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). As a research prototype, BHDL demonstrates the power of functional composition for PCB design but has a smaller ecosystem than Python-based alternatives.
 45→
 46→### 2.5 Polymorphic Blocks
 47→
 48→Polymorphic Blocks, developed by Lin et al. (2020), is a Python-embedded HDL that extends hierarchical block diagrams with polymorphism, typed ports carrying electrical parameters (voltage, current, thresholds), and generator blocks that programmatically instantiate subcircuits (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 2-4). Ports and links propagate parameters through a directed constraint graph and include assertions for correctness checking; generators can access solved values to automatically size components such as resistors from the E24 series (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 56-60, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5). The compiler lowers designs via refinement and generation steps and exports netlists for KiCad import, with deterministic component naming to support layout updates (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7). The project is open-source on GitHub and demonstrates how type systems and constraint propagation can provide meaningful automation for intermediate-level designers (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 10-11).
 49→
 50→### 2.6 JITX (Stanza-Based Programmatic PCB)
 51→
 52→JITX (jitx.com) is a commercial programmatic PCB design platform built on the Stanza programming language. Referenced in multiple academic works as JITPCB (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 27-30, li2021bhdlalucid pages 6-6), JITX embeds circuit construction primitives in a generator-style programming model, enabling parameterized design families with algorithmic placement, routing, and constraint-driven automation. It produces complete manufacturing outputs including Gerber files, BOMs, and pick-and-place data. JITX's integrated rule and constraint system is among the most capable code-native PCB verification flows. However, its proprietary licensing creates friction for open-hardware workflows, and the requirement to learn Stanza represents a barrier compared with Python-based alternatives (guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 129-132). JITX is commercially mature and targets professional engineering teams requiring high automation.
 53→
 54→### 2.7 PCBmodE
 55→
 56→PCBmodE (github.com/boldport/pcbmode) takes a unique approach by defining PCB geometry and artwork through JSON configuration files and SVG graphics. This makes it excellent for artistic or geometry-driven board designs where visual aesthetics matter. It produces Gerber manufacturing files from SVG-based board descriptions. However, its electrical abstraction is weaker than HDL-style tools—it lacks netlist-level schematic capture, DRC, or ERC capabilities. The project has seen limited recent activity but remains interesting as a purely text/data-driven PCB definition approach that is naturally Git-friendly.
 57→
 58→### 2.8 gEDA/gaf
 59→
 60→The gEDA project provides a traditional Unix-style open-source EDA suite with text-based schematics (gschem), scriptable netlisting utilities, and the PCB layout tool with Gerber export via gerbv. Files are text-based and CLI tools support scripted workflows, making gEDA compatible with version control and CI pipelines. However, the toolchain feels legacy compared with modern alternatives, and parametric design support is less ergonomic than newer HDL-based flows. Licensed under GPL, gEDA remains functional but has largely been superseded by KiCad for new open-hardware projects.
 61→
 62→### 2.9 Horizon EDA
 63→
 64→Horizon EDA (github.com/horizon-eda/horizon) is a GUI-first open-source EDA tool with strong DRC, manufacturing-output capabilities, and structured text-backed project data. While not a "code-CAD" tool per se, its project files are Git-manageable and it supports some scriptability and CLI-driven export. Horizon EDA excels at part and library management and is licensed under GPL-3.0. It bridges the gap between GUI-first design and version-controllable workflows.
 65→
 66→### 2.10 Edea
 67→
 68→Edea (github.com/edea-dev/edea) is a Python library for programmatically generating or manipulating KiCad project files. It operates as a tooling layer rather than a full EDA suite, enabling scripts to create, modify, or template KiCad schematics and PCB layouts. This makes it ideal for CI pipeline integration—for example, automated variant generation or BOM manipulation—while delegating ERC/DRC to KiCad itself. Edea is open-source and fits well into workflows where KiCad is the primary design tool but automation is needed around it.
 69→
 70→### 2.11 KiCad Python Scripting and kicad-cli
 71→
 72→KiCad, the dominant open-source PCB tool (GPL-3.0), offers extensive automation through its Python scripting API, action plugins, and the newer `kicad-cli` command-line interface. The Python API enables programmatic creation and manipulation of schematics, footprints, and board layouts; action plugins extend the GUI with custom functionality (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 97-100, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 97-100). The `kicad-cli` tool (introduced in KiCad 7+) supports headless export of Gerber files, drill files, BOMs, PDFs, STEP models, and schematic/PCB renderings—making it the key enabler for CI pipelines. KiCad's text-based file formats (`.kicad_sch`, `.kicad_pcb`) are Git-manageable, though diffs can be complex (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9). The HardOps framework demonstrated that CI pipelines can automate quality checks, artifact generation, and documentation for open-hardware projects, with KiCad as a central tool (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12).
 73→
 74→## 3. LLM-Based and AI/Generative Approaches
 75→
 76→Recent research has explored using large language models for text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB generation. The following table summarizes the key systems:
 77→
 78→| System | Approach | Intermediate Representation | Verification Method | Benchmark Size | Best Reported Performance | Key Limitations | Year/Status |
 79→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 80→| PCBSchemaGen (Zou et al.) | Training-free LLM pipeline for natural-language-to-schematic generation using domain-specific prompting, CoT, ICL, KG-guided constraints, and iterative feedback (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | SKiDL Python code, then KiCad schematic/layout artifacts (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4) | Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in ERC, knowledge-graph pin-role checks, topology verification via subgraph isomorphism/VF2, iterative localized feedback (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26) | 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks across digital, analog, and power domains (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | Hard-task success reported at 78.1%; easy-task success 93.3%; best model ~23k tokens/task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 min/task (~37× human speed) (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | Failures from misunderstanding pin functions and generating incorrect abstract topologies; depends on curated KG/component library (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26) | 2025 research prototype / open-source benchmark+code (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) |
 81→| HWE-Bench (Qiu et al.) | Execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design; prompts models through module decomposition, component allocation, local netlists, and global integration (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5) | Structured plain-text/JSON netlists derived from NL specs and datasheets; converted to SPICE/test representations for checking (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 2-3) | Dual verification: static rule checking on topology/protocol/pin properties plus dynamic LTspice transient simulation with threshold checks; pin-locking/resource-conflict simulation (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5) | 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8) | Best overall full-design pass rate only 8.15%; average static pass ~71.84%, dynamic ~58.65% (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2) | Major failures in context overload, role confusion, multiplexed-pin conflicts, and poor global physical reasoning; models often pass local/static checks but fail full design correctness (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8) | 2025 benchmark/evaluation framework |
 82→| PCEval (Song et al.) | Benchmark for physical-computing LLM tasks spanning circuit + code generation for Arduino-like projects; evaluates both logical and physical design ability (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Logical circuit descriptions and physical circuit layouts with associated code artifacts in simulation (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Automated simulation-based validation of logical correctness and physical feasibility, including pin-conflict and extraneous-component checks (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Not stated in gathered excerpt; covers multiple project complexities and 13 LLMs (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | No single percentage cited in gathered excerpt; qualitative result is that models do reasonably on code/logical circuit generation but struggle on breadboard-level physical layout (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Weakness on physical wiring/layout correctness despite better performance on code and logical circuits; oriented to physical-computing tasks rather than full PCB CAD (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | 2025 benchmark |
 83→| General LLM + SKiDL approaches | Use an LLM to synthesize executable Python-based schematic code from text, leveraging SKiDL because it is concise, code-native, and better aligned with LLMs than vendor schematic formats (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | SKiDL Python as the central executable IR; downstream conversion to KiCad schematics/PCB files (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | Typically combines SKiDL/ERC checks with downstream KiCad generation; stronger systems add datasheet-derived knowledge graphs, topological checks, and iterative feedback loops (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | No single standard benchmark; examples include PCBSchemaGen's 23-task suite and related evaluation frameworks like HWE-Bench/PCEval (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Most convincing evidence comes from PCBSchemaGen rather than generic SKiDL prompting; generic approaches remain highly variable and dataset-constrained (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | Limited by scarce open PCB data, vendor-specific capture formats, package/pin constraints, and insufficient physical verification if using only syntax/ERC or SPICE (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | 2023-2025 emerging pattern rather than a single mature tool (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) |
 84→
 85→
 86→*Table: This table compares the main recent LLM-based text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB research systems, their intermediate representations, verification strategies, benchmark scales, and limitations. It is useful for distinguishing promising constraint-guided approaches from broader benchmarks that reveal current reliability gaps.*
 87→
 88→**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al.) is a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses LLMs to generate SKiDL Python code from natural-language specifications. It builds a knowledge graph from IC datasheets encoding pin-role semantics (36 role types) and topological constraints, compressing per-device context from ~16k tokens to ~300 tokens (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4). Multi-stage verification combines SKiDL syntax/ERC checks with topology verification via the VF2 subgraph isomorphism algorithm (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6). On a 23-task benchmark spanning digital, analog, and power domains, PCBSchemaGen achieved 93.3% success on easy tasks and 78.1% on hard tasks, with a ~37× speedup over human designers (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2).
 89→
 90→**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al.) provides a more rigorous evaluation with 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains paired with 2,914 IC datasheets. It applies dual verification: static electrical rule checking and dynamic LTspice transient simulation (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5). The results are sobering: even the best model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieved only 8.15% overall correct-design pass rate, despite average static pass rates of ~72% (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2). Key failure modes include context overload with large component lists, MCU pin-multiplexing errors, and inability to maintain global logical consistency (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8).
 91→
 92→**PCEval** (Song et al.) benchmarks LLMs on physical computing tasks combining circuit and code generation for Arduino projects. Models performed reasonably on logical circuit design and code generation but struggled with physical breadboard-level layout, particularly pin-conflict avoidance (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3).
 93→
 94→These results indicate that while LLM-assisted schematic generation shows promise for constrained subtasks—especially when augmented with knowledge graphs, iterative feedback, and domain-specific verification—full end-to-end autonomous PCB design remains unreliable for production use.
 95→
 96→## 4. Trade-offs: GUI-Based EDA vs. Code-Based EDA vs. AI/Generative EDA
 97→
 98→**GUI-based EDA** (KiCad, Altium, EAGLE) excels at interactive layout optimization, complex trace routing, and visual inspection. Experienced designers achieve superior placement and routing quality through direct manipulation. However, GUI tools produce binary or semi-structured files that resist meaningful Git diffs, make parametric variant generation manual, and are difficult to drive from CI pipelines without significant scripting effort (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 12-13).
 99→
100→**Code-based EDA** (atopile, SKiDL, tscircuit, BHDL, JITX) treats electronics design as a programming activity with all attendant benefits: plain-text source control, meaningful diffs and code review, automated testing, parameterized reuse through functions and modules, and deterministic CI-generated outputs (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9). The trade-off is that placement and routing often require handoff to downstream tools (KiCad, FreeRouting) or are handled by less mature auto-placers (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). Lin and Hartmann identified confidence in code-generated hardware as a key challenge: unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly, so community mechanisms for marking subcircuits as "physically fabricated and tested" are important (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4).
101→
102→**AI/Generative EDA** offers the most ambitious vision—natural-language to manufactured board—but current capabilities fall short. The HWE-Bench results (8.15% full-design pass rate) demonstrate that LLMs lack the physical intuition and global consistency needed for reliable circuit design (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8). Targeted applications such as subcircuit suggestion, component selection, or datasheet extraction are more immediately practical (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 10-11, guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 20-24).
103→
104→## 5. Recommendations for an Open-Hardware, CI-Rendered Modular Dosing Device
105→
106→For a modular multi-powder dosing device—likely involving motor drivers, load-cell amplifiers, microcontroller modules, and communication interfaces as repeated building blocks—the following architecture is recommended:
107→
108→**Primary design tool: KiCad + atopile or SKiDL for schematic generation.** atopile's `.ato` files provide the cleanest "design-as-code" experience with native KiCad integration, Git-friendliness, and a growing package ecosystem. SKiDL offers a mature, well-documented alternative with the advantage of full Python ecosystem access and demonstrated compatibility with LLM-assisted generation workflows (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3). Both produce KiCad-compatible outputs.
109→
110→**CI pipeline: kicad-cli + GitHub/GitLab CI.** Following HardOps principles (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12), the pipeline should run ERC/DRC checks, generate Gerber/drill files, render board previews (SVG/PDF), produce BOMs, and publish release artifacts on each commit. The `kicad-cli` tool enables all of these operations headlessly. Docker containers with KiCad pre-installed ensure reproducible builds.
111→
112→**Modular reuse:** The dosing device's repeated channel modules (each with motor driver, load cell, valve control) should be defined as parameterized blocks—either atopile modules or SKiDL generator functions—instantiated N times with channel-specific parameters. Polymorphic Blocks' approach of abstract block types with typed ports and constraint propagation provides a strong conceptual model for this (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5).
113→
114→**Edea** can serve as a CI-layer tool for KiCad file manipulation tasks such as variant generation, BOM post-processing, or automated schematic transformations.
115→
116→**Layout:** For a production device, interactive KiCad layout with replication of channel modules (using hierarchical sheets and "connection room" replication) remains preferable to fully automated placement, which is not yet sufficiently mature in open-source tools for mixed-signal designs (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6).
117→
118→**Licensing alignment:** atopile (Apache-2.0), SKiDL (MIT), KiCad (GPL-3.0), Edea (open-source), and tscircuit (MIT) all support open-hardware distribution. JITX's proprietary model is less suitable for community-driven projects.
119→
120→## 6. Conclusion
121→
122→The design-as-code paradigm for electronics has matured substantially since 2020, with tools spanning custom HDLs (atopile), Python eDSLs (SKiDL, Polymorphic Blocks), functional languages (BHDL in Racket), React/TypeScript frameworks (tscircuit), and commercial platforms (JITX). These tools share the vision of raising abstraction through generators, type systems, and reusable libraries while producing version-controllable, CI-amenable artifacts (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2). LLM-based approaches show promise for constrained subtasks but remain unreliable for full-design autonomy (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2). For an open-hardware modular dosing device, a hybrid workflow combining code-generated schematics (atopile or SKiDL), KiCad-based layout/DRC, and CI automation via `kicad-cli` and HardOps principles offers the best balance of reproducibility, manufacturability, and community accessibility.
