messages.content:
1→Question: Find recent (2019-2025) peer-reviewed and arXiv academic publications on automated and generative schematic / circuit-topology synthesis and netlist generation for electronic design. Cover: (a) machine-learning and generative models for analog and mixed-signal circuit topology generation, (b) LLM-based and agentic generation of schematics, netlists, and circuit descriptions (e.g., LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, Atelier/Artisan-style analog agents, schematic-from-spec systems), (c) graph-neural-network and reinforcement-learning approaches to circuit synthesis and device sizing, and (d) constraint-/specification-driven topology selection. For each paper give: full citation (authors, year, venue, volume, pages, DOI/arXiv id), a 3-5 sentence summary, and why it is relevant to building a code-based generative pipeline for the control/driver electronics of an open-source modular multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end). Note capabilities and limitations (validity of generated circuits, manufacturability, hallucination, evaluation). Aim for ~10-15 strong references.
  2→
  3→# Automated and Generative Schematic/Circuit-Topology Synthesis and Netlist Generation for Electronic Design: A Literature Survey (2019–2025)
  4→
  5→## Overview
  6→
  7→This survey covers 15 key references spanning four methodological categories relevant to building a code-based generative pipeline for the control/driver electronics of an open-source modular multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller interfaces, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end amplifiers). The references are organized into: (a) machine-learning and generative models for analog/mixed-signal circuit topology generation, (b) LLM-based and agentic generation of schematics and netlists, (c) graph-neural-network (GNN) and reinforcement-learning (RL) approaches to circuit synthesis and device sizing, and (d) constraint-/specification-driven topology selection.
  8→
  9→The following table provides a compact overview of all 15 references:
 10→
 11→| Reference # | Authors (Year) | Title | Venue | Category (a/b/c/d) | Key Method | Key Results/Metrics | Limitations |
 12→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 13→| 1 | Chang et al. (2024) | LaMAGIC | arXiv:2407.18269 | a,b | SFT on Flan-T5 for analog topology generation using adjacency-matrix and float-input formulations | Up to 96% success at tolerance 0.01; transfer from 3-5 component pretraining to 6-component circuits; power-converter focus (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 5-6) | Small circuits only; synthetic data and simulated evaluation; limited component diversity (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5) |
 14→| 2 | Chang et al. (2025) | LaMAGIC2 | arXiv:2506.10235 | a,b | SFCI representation reduces sequence length from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) and improves numeric precision handling | 34% higher success than LaMAGIC under tight tolerance and 10x lower MSE; shorter token sequences and better transferability (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5) | Still focused on power-converter topology generation; not broadly shown on mixed-signal control electronics (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2) |
 15→| 3 | Gao et al. (2025) | AnalogGenie | arXiv:2503.00205 | a | GPT-based pin-level graph generation with Eulerian-circuit encoding over 3,350 topologies across 11 circuit types | Discovers unseen and larger-scale topologies; reports zero-shot generation beyond training distribution; ~70x data augmentation via multiple serializations (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 15-19) | Requires a large curated dataset and custom representation pipeline; weak evidence for direct spec conditioning (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 1-2) |
 16→| 4 | Dong et al. (2023) | CktGNN | arXiv:2308.16406 | a,c | Two-level nested GNN with subgraph basis plus VAE latent generation; Open Circuit Benchmark with 10K op-amps | ~91.9% valid-circuit rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson r≈0.806; outperforms strong GNN baselines and reported human designs (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 9-11) | Benchmark is op-amp-centric; uses idealized graph abstractions and component models, limiting manufacturable transistor-level realism (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 2-4) |
 17→| 5 | Lai et al. (2025) | AnalogCoder | arXiv:2405.14918 | b | Training-free LLM agent generating PySpice code with domain prompts, feedback loop, and circuit tool library | Solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, beating GPT-4o; checks requirements, simulation, operating point, DC sweep, and function (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28) | Struggles on complex circuits; prioritizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; still needs external simulation to catch invalid outputs (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 9-11, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13) |
 18→| 6 | Zhang et al. (2025) | AnalogXpert | ISEDA 2025, pp. 772-777; arXiv:2412.19824 | b,d | Training-free LLM agent with subcircuit library, CoT prompting, in-context examples, iterative proofreading, and SPICE output | 40% synthetic success and 23% real-data success versus GPT-4o at 3% and 3%; more practical SPICE-level output than idealized graph methods (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 1-2, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10) | Small real dataset; connection mistakes and wrong block choices still common; needs richer real-data corpora or fine-tuned local models (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10) |
 19→| 7 | Vijayaraghavan et al. (2024) | CircuitSynth | IEEE LAD 2024, pp. 1-6 | b | Two-phase pipeline: LLM netlist generation plus RoBERTa validity classifier; Gumbel-softmax refinement toward valid circuits | Uses 862,606-example power-converter dataset; validity classifier F1≈92%; SPICE-validated validity about 0.713-0.728 for best variants, i.e. ~72% (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 2-3, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 3-4) | Restricted to power converters and validity-centric evaluation; no explicit broad spec-conditioned AMS block generation (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6) |
 20→| 8 | Chen et al. (2025) | MenTeR | IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124-132 | b,d | Multi-agent workflow with PI, Circuit, and Testbench agents; DA-RAG, Chain-of-Stage reasoning, execution checks, iterative refinement | GPT-4o backbone reached 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5; supports end-to-end netlist and testbench generation (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5) | Human-in-the-loop still needed for complex systems; subcircuit-definition and missing-wire errors remain at system level (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8) |
 21→| 9 | Wang et al. (2025) | AnalogSAGE | arXiv:2512.22435 | b,c,d | Self-evolving multi-agent workflow with topology selection, refinement, parameter optimization, stratified memory, RAG, and Bayesian optimization grounded by simulation | Reported 10x overall pass-rate improvement, 48x Pass@1 improvement, and 4x smaller parameter search space on SKY130 op-amp tasks (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 4-5) | Limited to op-amp designs and SKY130 environment; late 2025 arXiv work with little independent validation so far (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2) |
 22→| 10 | Bhandari et al. (2024) | Masala-CHAI | arXiv:2411.14299 | b | Automated schematic-to-SPICE dataset creation from textbook diagrams using YOLOv8, net extraction, prompt tuning, and LLM verification | Fine-tuning on Masala-CHAI improves AnalogCoder Pass@1 by 46%; ~7,500 netlists with GED-based structural verification (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 7-9) | Component confusion such as NMOS versus PMOS and weak transistor-level net detection remain major issues; net detection can be ~45% accurate without stronger CV support (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, tao2024amsnetnetlistdataset pages 1-2) |
 23→| 11 | Settaluri et al. (2020) | AutoCkt | arXiv:2001.01808 | c | PPO-based deep RL for analog sizing across multiple target specs with transfer from schematic to post-layout environments | 96.3% target convergence on unseen specs, ~40x faster than genetic algorithms, and 40 LVS-passed op-amps in layout-aware experiments (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6) | Fixed-topology sizing only; requires simulator access and substantial environment engineering; not a topology generator (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6) |
 24→| 12 | Zhao and Zhang (2022) | DRL Circuit Structure Synthesis | DATE 2022, pp. 1157-1160 | c,d | DRL topology synthesis from predefined building blocks with policy-gradient network, hash-table caching, and symbolic analysis filtering | Early RL topology-synthesis framework; uses GPDD symbolic filtering and SPICE-based NSGA-II verification; explores novel structures with lower computation than older methods (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4) | Constrained by building-block library and state encoding; stochastic results; runs can take 2-9 hours and still need downstream sizing (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-2) |
 25→| 13 | Choi et al. (2024) | MA-Opt | IEEE TCAS-I 71(5):2045-2056 | c | Multi-actor RL for analog sizing with shared elite set, critic surrogate, and cooperative near-sampling | Up to 34% better FoM and target metrics than DNN-Opt at equal simulation budget; improves exploration and sample efficiency (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2) | Sizing only, not topology synthesis; depends on an already valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2) |
 26→| 14 | Poddar et al. (2024) | Data-Driven Synthesizer | DATE 2024, pp. 1-6 | a,d | Modular parameterized topology library, VAE synthetic data augmentation, binary-search and synthetic ranking, plus DNN-Opt verification and sizing | Evaluated on 360+ OPAMP topologies and 540K+ data points; generates optimal-sized topology netlists in about 10 minutes; synthetic data helps when real samples are sparse (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 5-6, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4) | Requires significant one-time simulation and data collection; limited to OPAMP and OTA families in curated libraries (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4) |
 27→| 15 | Guo et al. (2019) | GAN Circuit Synthesis | AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum | a | GAN, WGAN, and improved WGAN generation of circuit-topology adjacency encodings for frequency-response and low-pass-filter synthesis | Improved WGAN reached up to 42.66% feasibility in the frequency-response case and beat GAN, DCGAN, and WGAN baselines (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17) | Feasibility remains relatively low; graph-isomorphism and representation issues persist; no spec-conditioned or manufacturability-aware generation loop (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 2-4, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17) |
 28→
 29→
 30→*Table: This table summarizes 15 influential 2019-2025 references on automated and generative circuit-topology synthesis, netlist generation, and analog design agents. It is useful for comparing methods, results, and practical limitations when building a code-based electronics design pipeline.*
 31→
 32→---
 33→
 34→## Detailed Reference Summaries
 35→
 36→### Category (a): ML and Generative Models for Analog Circuit Topology Generation
 37→
 38→**1. Chang, C.-C., Shen, Y., Fan, S., Li, J., Zhang, S., Cao, N., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2024). "LaMAGIC: Language-Model-based Topology Generation for Analog Integrated Circuits." arXiv:2407.18269.**
 39→
 40→LaMAGIC pioneers the use of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a Flan-T5 (248M parameters) encoder-decoder model to generate analog circuit topologies from user-specified performance targets (e.g., voltage conversion ratio, efficiency) in a single forward pass (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 2-3). The system represents circuits as graphs encoded via adjacency matrices and develops multiple input/output formulations — including a float-input adjacency-matrix formulation (FM) — that align with the autoregressive nature of language models. Experimental results on power converter circuits demonstrate a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01 (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2). The model is pretrained on 3–5 component circuits and transferred to 6-component designs using a dataset of 76K sampled circuits (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5). **Relevance to dosing device:** Demonstrates that specification-to-topology generation is feasible via LMs, a paradigm applicable to generating power converter sub-circuits (e.g., stepper driver power stages). **Limitations:** Restricted to small power converter topologies (3–6 passive/switching components); synthetic evaluation only; no direct applicability to mixed-signal blocks like load-cell amplifiers (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5).
 41→
 42→**2. Chang, C.-C., Lin, W.-H., Shen, Y., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2025). "LaMAGIC2: Advanced Circuit Formulations for Language Model-Based Analog Topology Generation." arXiv:2506.10235.**
 43→
 44→LaMAGIC2 introduces the Succinct Float-Input Canonical Formulation with Identifier (SFCI), which reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|²) to O(|V|) and adds component-type identifiers for better differentiation (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2). This yields 34% higher success rates under tight tolerance (0.01) and 10× lower MSE compared to the original LaMAGIC, with substantially shorter output sequences (~36–41 tokens vs. ~85–105) (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5). **Relevance:** Improved scalability and precision make this formulation more practical for generating larger circuit blocks. **Limitations:** Still confined to power converter topologies; O(|V|) scaling, while improved, has not been demonstrated on complex mixed-signal circuits (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5).
 45→
 46→**3. Gao, J., Cao, W., Yang, J., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogGenie: A Generative Engine for Automatic Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies." arXiv:2503.00205.**
 47→
 48→AnalogGenie is a GPT-based generative engine that represents circuits at the device-pin level using an Eulerian-circuit encoding of directed graphs, trained on a dataset of 3,350 distinct topologies across 11 circuit families (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4). The pin-level representation captures low-level design details and scales to larger graphs better than adjacency-matrix methods. Data augmentation via multiple Eulerian serializations per topology yields ~70× more training data (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7). AnalogGenie demonstrates zero-shot generation of circuit types not present in training (e.g., transconductance amplifiers) and produces novel topologies validated in Cadence Virtuoso, including op-amps (GBW=12 MHz), DC converters (efficiency=0.95), and bandgap references (TC=3 ppm/°C) (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 15-19). **Relevance:** The ability to discover novel topologies across diverse circuit families (including amplifiers and references) is directly applicable to generating load-cell front-end and voltage reference sub-circuits. **Limitations:** Requires a large curated dataset; specification conditioning is indirect (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 1-2).
 49→
 50→**4. Guo, T., Herber, D. R., and Allison, J. T. (2019). "Circuit Synthesis Using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)." AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum. DOI: 10.2514/6.2019-2350.**
 51→
 52→This early work applies GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) to generate circuit topologies encoded as adjacency matrices. Using datasets of 43K–123K enumerated topologies for frequency-response matching and low-pass filter realizability, the improved WGAN achieved the highest feasibility rate of up to 42.66% after 50,000 training iterations (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17). **Relevance:** Establishes the foundational concept of using generative models for circuit topology search, relevant to exploring filter and signal-conditioning topologies. **Limitations:** Relatively low feasibility rates; graph isomorphism problems in fixed-size adjacency representations; no specification conditioning or manufacturability awareness (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 2-4, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17).
 53→
 54→**5. Poddar, S., Budak, A., Zhao, L., Hsu, C.-H., Maji, S., Zhu, K., Jia, Y., and Pan, D. Z. (2024). "A Data-Driven Analog Circuit Synthesizer with Automatic Topology Selection and Sizing." DATE 2024, pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.23919/DATE58400.2024.10546840.**
 55→
 56→This work presents a modular, data-driven framework that combines a parameterized OPAMP topology library (~4,000 valid topologies, ~360 curated), VAE-based synthetic data augmentation, and DNN-Opt sizing to automatically select and size topologies from performance specifications (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4). The system generates optimal-sized netlists in approximately 10 minutes using over 540K data points (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 5-6). **Relevance:** The modular library approach and specification-driven selection are directly applicable to choosing amplifier topologies for load-cell signal conditioning. **Limitations:** Requires a costly one-time data-collection effort; limited to OPAMP/OTA families (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4).
 57→
 58→---
 59→
 60→### Category (b): LLM-Based and Agentic Generation of Schematics and Netlists
 61→
 62→**6. Lai, Y., Lee, S., Chen, G., Poddar, S., Hu, M., Pan, D. Z., and Luo, P. (2025). "AnalogCoder: Analog Circuit Design via Training-Free Code Generation." arXiv:2405.14918.**
 63→
 64→AnalogCoder is the first training-free LLM agent that reframes analog circuit design as Python (PySpice) code generation (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). It uses domain-specific prompts, a four-stage feedback loop (requirement checks, simulation/operating-point checks, DC sweep checks, functional checks), and a circuit tool library that archives successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). AnalogCoder solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, outperforming standard GPT-4o (15 solved) and Llama-3 (11 solved) (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). **Relevance:** The PySpice code-generation paradigm and modular sub-circuit library are directly transferable to generating driver electronics: one could encode stepper-driver half-bridges, motor H-bridges, or instrumentation amplifier blocks as library components and compose them via LLM-generated code. **Limitations:** Struggles with highly complex circuits; emphasizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; requires external SPICE simulation for validation; a later evaluation (AnalogCoder-Pro) showed only 10% pass rate on harder specification-driven benchmarks (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 9-11, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 6-7).
 65→
 66→**7. Zhang, H., Sun, S., Lin, Y., Wang, R., and Bian, J. (2025). "AnalogXpert: Automating Analog Topology Synthesis by Incorporating Circuit Design Expertise into Large Language Models." ISEDA 2025, pp. 772–777. arXiv:2412.19824.**
 67→
 68→AnalogXpert formulates topology synthesis as subcircuit-level SPICE code generation and incorporates circuit design expertise through a subcircuit library, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, in-context learning examples, and iterative human-experience-based proofreading (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 1-2). On a benchmark of 30 real and 2,000 synthetic tasks, AnalogXpert achieved 40% and 23% success rates respectively, markedly outperforming GPT-4o at 3%/3% (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10). **Relevance:** The SPICE-level output and subcircuit decomposition align well with practical PCB-level design (e.g., decomposing a motor driver into gate driver, half-bridge, current sense subcircuits). **Limitations:** Connection errors and wrong block selections remain common failure modes; the real dataset is small (30 tasks) (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10).
 69→
 70→**8. Vijayaraghavan, P., Shi, L., Degan, E., and Zhang, X. (2024). "CircuitSynth: Leveraging Large Language Models for Circuit Topology Synthesis." IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.1109/LAD62341.2024.10691716.**
 71→
 72→CircuitSynth introduces a two-phase pipeline: Phase 1 fine-tunes an LLM to generate netlist-encoded topologies autoregressively, and Phase 2 applies a RoBERTa-based validity classifier (F1 ≈ 92%) with Gumbel-softmax refinement to steer generation toward valid circuits (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 2-3). Trained on 862,606 power-converter examples, the best models achieved ~71–73% SPICE-validated topology validity (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 3-4, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6). **Relevance:** The validity-classifier refinement concept could be adapted to filter LLM-generated netlists for driver electronics against electrical rule checks. **Limitations:** Restricted to power converters; no specification-conditioned generation (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6).
 73→
 74→**9. Chen, P.-H., Lin, Y.-S., Lee, W., Leu, T.-Y., Hsu, P.-H., Dissanayake, A., Oh, S., and Chiu, C.-S. (2025). "MenTeR: A Fully-Automated Multi-Agent Workflow for End-to-End RF/Analog Circuits Netlist Design." IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124–132. arXiv:2505.22990.**
 75→
 76→MenTeR deploys a multi-agent architecture comprising a Primary Investigator (PI) agent for specification understanding, a Circuit Agent for netlist synthesis via Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning, and a Testbench Agent for simulation validation using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG) (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5). With GPT-4o as backbone, MenTeR achieved 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5, supporting end-to-end design from specification to validated PySpice netlist and testbench (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8). **Relevance:** The multi-agent spec-to-netlist-to-testbench pipeline is the closest paradigm to what would be needed for an automated dosing-device electronics generator — one agent interprets requirements (e.g., "4 stepper channels, 24V, load-cell excitation"), another generates netlists, and a third validates via simulation. **Limitations:** AI-generated nets can contain subcircuit definition errors and missing wires at system level, necessitating human-in-the-loop review for complex designs (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8).
 77→
 78→**10. Wang, Z., Gao, J., Fu, W., Guo, X., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogSAGE: Self-Evolving Analog Design Multi-Agents with Stratified Memory and Grounded Experience." arXiv:2512.22435.**
 79→
 80→AnalogSAGE coordinates three sequential agent stages — topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization — within a closed-loop, simulation-grounded workflow augmented by four-layer stratified memory for long-horizon reasoning and cross-task knowledge transfer (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 2-3, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2). Using SKY130 PDK and ngspice, AnalogSAGE reports a 10× overall pass-rate improvement and 48× Pass@1 improvement over baselines, with Bayesian optimization for parameter tuning (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 4-5). **Relevance:** The self-evolving, simulation-grounded approach with memory-based learning could enable iterative improvement of driver circuit designs across multiple dosing channels. **Limitations:** Currently demonstrated only on op-amp designs in SKY130; recent preprint with limited independent validation (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2).
 81→
 82→**11. Bhandari, J., Bhat, V., He, Y., Garg, S., Rahmani, H., and Karri, R. (2024). "Masala-CHAI: A Large-Scale SPICE Netlist Dataset for Analog Circuits by Harnessing AI." arXiv:2411.14299.**
 83→
 84→Masala-CHAI provides an automated pipeline for extracting SPICE netlists from circuit schematic images in textbooks, using YOLOv8 for component detection and deep Hough-transform priors for net recognition, followed by LLM-based netlist generation and verification (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 3-4). The open-sourced dataset of ~7,500 netlists from 10 textbooks enables LLM fine-tuning; models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI improved AnalogCoder's Pass@1 by 46% (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3). Structural correctness is verified via Graph Edit Distance (GED), achieving 100% similarity with AMSNet reference netlists (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 7-9). **Relevance:** The dataset and pipeline could be extended to extract and catalog common control electronics circuits (H-bridges, stepper drivers, instrumentation amplifiers) from open-source hardware documentation, creating training data for a dosing-device-specific generator. **Limitations:** Component confusion (NMOS vs. PMOS) and weak transistor-level net detection (~45% accuracy without CV support) remain significant challenges (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, tao2024amsnetnetlistdataset pages 1-2).
 85→
 86→---
 87→
 88→### Category (c): GNN and RL Approaches to Circuit Synthesis and Device Sizing
 89→
 90→**12. Dong, Z., Cao, W., Zhang, M., Tao, D., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2023). "CktGNN: Circuit Graph Neural Network for Electronic Design Automation." arXiv:2308.16406.**
 91→
 92→CktGNN uses a two-level (nested) GNN architecture where inner GNNs encode subgraph-basis components and an outer directed GNN performs message passing over the transformed circuit DAG (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 1-2, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 4-6). Integrated into a VAE framework, CktGNN jointly addresses topology generation and device sizing. The authors release the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) with 10K distinct operational amplifiers. In experiments, CktGNN achieves ~91.9% valid-circuit generation rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson correlation r ≈ 0.806, and outperforms human expert designs in Bayesian-optimization-guided search (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 9-11). **Relevance:** The GNN+VAE approach to joint topology-and-sizing optimization is applicable to amplifier blocks in a dosing device (e.g., load-cell instrumentation amplifiers, analog front-ends). The OCB benchmark and open-source code support reproducible research. **Limitations:** Benchmark is op-amp-centric with idealized component models; not directly demonstrated on mixed-signal driver circuits (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 2-4).
 93→
 94→**13. Settaluri, K., Haj-Ali, A., Huang, Q., Hakhamaneshi, K., and Nikolic, B. (2020). "AutoCkt: Deep Reinforcement Learning of Analog Circuit Designs." arXiv:2001.01808.**
 95→
 96→AutoCkt formulates analog circuit sizing as a multi-objective RL problem using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), where the agent observes simulated performance and target specifications and iteratively adjusts device parameters (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 2-3). Trained on only 50 target specifications, AutoCkt generalizes to 96.3% of 1,000 unseen targets with an average of 27 simulations to converge — approximately 40× faster than genetic algorithms (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5). Transfer learning from schematic to post-layout (PEX) environments produced 40 LVS-passed operational amplifier layouts in TSMC 16nm (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 6-6, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6). **Relevance:** Once a topology is selected for a dosing-device sub-circuit (e.g., a transimpedance amplifier for load-cell readout), AutoCkt-style RL sizing can rapidly optimize component values to meet gain, bandwidth, and noise specifications. **Limitations:** Fixed-topology sizing only; requires access to a SPICE simulator and environment engineering; not a topology generator (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2).
 97→
 98→**14. Zhao, Z. and Zhang, L. (2022). "Deep Reinforcement Learning for Analog Circuit Structure Synthesis." DATE 2022, pp. 1157–1160. DOI: 10.23919/DATE54114.2022.9774699.**
 99→
100→This work is among the first to apply deep RL directly to analog circuit topology synthesis (rather than just sizing). A policy-gradient neural network (PGNN) incrementally constructs topologies by selecting building blocks from a predefined library, with hash tables and GPDD symbolic analysis used to efficiently filter and evaluate candidates before SPICE-based NSGA-II verification (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4). The method requires substantially less computation than brute-force enumeration approaches like MOJITO (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4). **Relevance:** The building-block-based topology construction paradigm could be adapted for driver electronics by defining blocks such as half-bridges, gate drivers, current mirrors, and feedback networks. **Limitations:** Constrained by the predefined building-block library; stochastic results requiring multiple runs (2–9 hours each); downstream sizing still needed (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-2).
101→
102→**15. Choi, Y., Park, S., Choi, M., Lee, K., and Kang, S. (2024). "MA-Opt: Reinforcement Learning-Based Analog Circuit Optimization Using Multi-Actors." IEEE TCAS-I, 71(5):2045–2056. DOI: 10.1109/TCSI.2024.3356582.**
103→
104→MA-Opt uses multiple parallel RL actors with a shared elite-solution memory and a cooperative near-sampling strategy to optimize analog circuit device parameters. A critic network serves as a surrogate simulator to reduce expensive SPICE calls (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). MA-Opt achieves up to 34% better Figure-of-Merit than DNN-Opt at the same simulation budget and consistently satisfies all given performance constraints (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). **Relevance:** Multi-actor RL sizing is applicable to optimizing component values in motor driver and signal-conditioning circuits where multiple performance targets (efficiency, thermal, bandwidth) must be simultaneously satisfied. **Limitations:** Sizing-only; requires a valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2).
105→
106→---
107→
108→## Cross-Cutting Analysis: Capabilities, Limitations, and Relevance to a Dosing-Device Electronics Pipeline
109→
110→### Capabilities
111→The surveyed methods collectively demonstrate that: (1) LM-based approaches can achieve high success rates (up to 96%) for specification-conditioned topology generation of well-defined circuit families (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2); (2) training-free LLM agents like AnalogCoder and MenTeR can generate functional PySpice/SPICE netlists with multi-stage feedback verification, solving 20+/24 benchmark tasks (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8); (3) GNN+VAE methods enable joint topology-sizing optimization with >91% validity rates (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20); and (4) RL-based sizing converges to meet specifications on 96%+ of unseen targets with 40× speedup over genetic algorithms (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5).
112→
113→### Hallucination and Validity
114→A recurring challenge across LLM-based methods is the generation of structurally invalid or electrically incorrect circuits. AnalogCoder requires multi-stage simulation-based feedback to catch floating nodes and incorrect operating points (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13). MenTeR found that AI-generated netlists sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet contained errors upon schematic inspection, including incorrectly defined subcircuits and missing wire connections (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8). AnalogXpert reports persistent connection errors and wrong block selections even with iterative proofreading (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10). These findings underscore the necessity of SPICE-level simulation verification in any generative pipeline.
115→
116→### Manufacturability
117→Most methods operate at the schematic level and do not address layout parasitics, PCB-level constraints, or component availability (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 11-12, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13). AutoCkt is a notable exception, demonstrating transfer to post-layout (PEX) environments with LVS-passed designs (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6). For a dosing device using discrete, off-the-shelf components (DRV8825 stepper drivers, HX711 load-cell ADCs, etc.), the gap between schematic-level generation and a manufacturable BOM/PCB is substantial — generated topologies would need to be mapped to specific component footprints, validated against datasheets, and checked for thermal and EMC compliance.
118→
119→### Relevance to Dosing-Device Control Electronics
120→The control electronics for a multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end) represent a system-level design challenge that spans digital control logic and multiple analog/mixed-signal sub-circuits. The most directly applicable paradigms are: (1) **Multi-agent agentic workflows** (MenTeR, AnalogSAGE) that decompose a system specification into sub-blocks and generate, validate, and iterate on each block's netlist (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2); (2) **Code-generation agents** (AnalogCoder) that produce executable PySpice code from natural-language descriptions, enabling composition of modular sub-circuits such as H-bridges and instrumentation amplifiers (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2); (3) **Specification-driven topology selectors** (Poddar et al.) that rank candidate topologies from a parameterized library against target specifications and automatically size them (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2); and (4) **RL-based optimizers** (AutoCkt, MA-Opt) for downstream component-value tuning once topologies are selected (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). A practical pipeline would likely combine these approaches: an LLM agent interprets system requirements and selects/generates topology candidates, a GNN or RL module optimizes device parameters, and simulation-in-the-loop verification catches hallucinated or invalid designs before output.
messages.name: read

messages.content:
1→Question: Survey the datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation methodologies used in machine-learning and generative electronic design automation (EDA) as of 2025, spanning both PCB-level and chip-level design. Cover: (a) circuit / netlist / schematic datasets and benchmark suites (e.g., VerilogEval, RTLLM, MCNC/ISPD/ICCAD placement and routing benchmarks, CircuitNet, the Open Circuit Benchmark / analog topology datasets, any open KiCad/PCB corpora), (b) metrics for placement, routing, schematic generation, and HDL generation (wirelength, congestion, DRC violations, functional pass rate, synthesizability), and (c) the gaps and challenges in evaluating generative circuit/PCB design (lack of standardized, manufacturable, open PCB datasets; reproducibility concerns). For each dataset/benchmark give a full citation, a 3-5 sentence description, license/availability, and its relevance to evaluating a generative pipeline for open-hardware control PCBs. Aim for ~10-15 references.
 2→
 3→# Datasets, Benchmarks, and Evaluation Methodologies for Machine-Learning and Generative EDA (2025 Survey)
 4→
 5→## 1. Introduction
 6→
 7→The rapid application of large language models (LLMs) and other ML methods to electronic design automation (EDA) has created a pressing need for standardized datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation metrics spanning the full design stack—from HDL code generation through chip physical design to printed circuit board (PCB) schematic and layout synthesis. This survey catalogues the principal resources available as of 2025 and identifies critical gaps, with emphasis on their relevance to evaluating generative pipelines for open-hardware control PCBs.
 8→
 9→The following table summarizes the major datasets and benchmarks identified in this survey:
10→
11→| Dataset/Benchmark Name | Citation (author-year) | Domain | Scale | Key Metrics | Availability (URL/license) | Relevance to Open-Hardware PCB Pipeline |
12→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
13→| VerilogEval | Liu et al. 2023 | HDL Generation | 156 HDLBits problems; machine/human subsets | Functional correctness via pass@k (commonly pass@1/5/10); simulation against golden solutions | GitHub: NVlabs/verilog-eval; open-source benchmark, license not stated in retrieved evidence (pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3, liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4, liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 1-2) | Useful for controller/FPGA/MCU-support logic generation that may accompany open control PCBs, but evaluates RTL only—not schematics, BOMs, footprints, or manufacturable PCB constraints. |
14→| RTLLM / RTLLM 2.0 | Lu et al. 2024; Liu et al. 2024 | HDL Generation | 30 designs in RTLLM; 50 designs in RTLLM 2.0 | Syntax goal, functionality goal, design-quality goal (PPA after synthesis/layout); testbench pass/fail | GitHub: hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM and related OpenLLM-RTL repos; open-source intent, license not stated in retrieved evidence (lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3, lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 3-4, liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 1-2, liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 2-3) | More relevant than VerilogEval when an open-hardware PCB pipeline includes synthesizable digital peripherals or glue logic; still misses board-level connectivity, ERC/DRC, connectors, and component/package realism. |
15→| CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems) | Pinckney et al. 2025 | HDL Generation | 783 problems across 13 task categories | pass@1 for code-generation tasks; BLEU and LLM-judge scoring for comprehension-style tasks | Open-source benchmark/infrastructure reported by authors; repository/open tooling stated, license not stated in retrieved evidence (from paper search results) | Valuable for evaluating agentic HDL workflows beyond one-shot generation, especially verification/debug loops relevant to mixed HW/SW control systems on open boards. |
16→| VeriContaminated | Wang et al. 2025 | HDL Generation / Evaluation Methodology | Contamination study over VerilogEval and RTLLM across many LLMs | Contamination-rate metrics using CCD, Min-K% Prob, Dolos-style analyses; fairness vs quality tradeoff | Open-sourced code on GitHub per paper; DOI/arXiv available (wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2) | Critical meta-benchmark for any open-hardware generative pipeline: reminds evaluators that apparent HDL success may be inflated by benchmark leakage, undermining reproducibility and trust. |
17→| CircuitNet / CircuitNet 2.0 | Chai et al. 2022; Jiang et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing | Original CircuitNet: 10,242 post-flow layouts from 12,960 backend runs, 28nm; CircuitNet 2.0: 10K+ samples, 14nm FinFET, CPU/GPU/AI-class designs | Congestion: NRMSE, SSIM; DRC/IR-drop: ROC-AUC and PR-AUC; labels include congestion, DRC/DRV hotspots, IR-drop, timing/routability | https://circuitnet.github.io/ ; open-source dataset, explicit license not stated in retrieved evidence (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 1-2, jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 1-3, chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 2-2) | Strongest open chip-physical-design dataset family for ML baselines. Indirectly relevant to open control PCBs by informing placement/routing evaluation thinking, but not directly transferable to PCB manufacturing files or board-level constraints. |
18→| ISPD / ICCAD Contest Suites | Various, 2005–2025 | Placement-Routing | Multi-year contest benchmark families across placement, routability, detailed placement, timing-driven placement, FPGA macro placement, global routing | HPWL/wirelength, congestion/routability, timing slack (WNS/TNS), legality, runtime; contest-specific objective functions | Conference archives / contest sites; generally public benchmark files, licenses vary by contest (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 5-6, kahng2021advancingplacement pages 2-3, bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 3-4, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9) | Essential baselines for chip-floorplanning and routing evaluation culture. Helpful as methodological precedent for PCB benchmark design, but not a substitute for open PCB corpora. |
19→| EDALearn | Pan et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing / End-to-End EDA | Holistic RTL-to-signoff suite across FreePDK45 and ASAP7; diverse public designs from IWLS, Chipyard/BOOM, OpenCores, ISCAS, ITC’99, etc. | Standardized evaluation for routability prediction, IR-drop prediction, cross-stage analysis; image-like and vector features | Open-source benchmark suite reported by authors; exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 1-2, pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 3-4) | Highly relevant as a template for reproducible end-to-end benchmarking. For open-hardware PCBs, it suggests how a board benchmark should connect source design intent to final manufacturable artifacts. |
20→| EDA-Schema-V2 | Shrestha et al. 2025 | Placement-Routing / Digital Physical Design | ~7,800 instances from 18 IWLS’05 circuits across 4 open PDKs (Sky130, Nangate45, IHP SG13G2, ASAP7) | Timing, power, area, routing/QoR prediction tasks; stage-aware benchmark metrics and baseline leaderboards | GitHub release reported; open schema/dataset, exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 1-3, shrestha2605edaschemav2amultimodal pages 28-29) | Particularly relevant methodologically: multimodal, stage-aware, reproducible, and open. A strong model for how an open PCB benchmark could combine schematic, layout, nets, images, and fabrication outputs. |
21→| AnalogGym | Li et al. 2024 | Analog | 30 circuit topologies in 5 categories: amplifiers, LDOs, sensing front ends, voltage references, PLLs | Multi-objective analog metrics over PVT: gain, bandwidth, power, noise, area; DC/AC/PZ/transient analyses | GitHub: CODA-Team/AnalogGym; open-source suite/documentation reported, exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (li2024analoggymanopen pages 2-3, li2024analoggymanopen pages 1-2, li2024analoggymanopen pages 3-4) | Very relevant for analog portions of control PCBs (power rails, sensing front ends). However, it evaluates IC-level analog synthesis, not discrete-component PCB schematics or board manufacturability. |
22→| AICircuit | Mehradfar et al. 2024 | Analog | 7 basic circuits + 2 heterogeneous wireless transceiver systems | Power consumption, bandwidth, gain, conversion gain, IF swing, noise figure, oscillation frequency, output power, phase noise, tuning range | Open-sourced data/code on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 2-4, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 6-7, mehradfar2024aicircuitamultilevel pages 1-2) | Relevant for learning analog performance mappings and inverse design ideas that could inform board-level analog block selection, but not directly a PCB schematic/layout benchmark. |
23→| PCBSchemaGen | Zou et al. 2025 | PCB | 23 PCB schematic-generation tasks; curated library with 18 symbols, 21 footprints, 14 datasheets | Pass@k (k=1,5), topology verification via subgraph isomorphism, ERC/syntax checks, semantic constraint checks, executable SKiDL/KiCad-ready outputs | Open-sourced KG/benchmark on GitHub per paper; exact license not stated in retrieved evidence (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | The most directly relevant benchmark for an open-hardware control-PCB generative pipeline: board-level, datasheet-grounded, KiCad/SKiDL-compatible, and checks topology rather than text similarity alone. |
24→| TuRTLe | Garcia-Gasulla et al. 2025 | HDL Generation / Unified Evaluation | Unified framework integrating benchmarks such as VerilogEval and RTLLM | STX (syntax), FNC (functional), SYN (synthesis) as PASS@1 stages; post-synthesis PPA-score from OpenLANE/SKY130A | Open-source framework reported in paper; exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4) | Useful because it adds synthesis-aware and PPA-aware evaluation, closer to practical deployment. Analogous board-level frameworks should similarly add ERC/DRC/BOM/manufacturability checks. |
25→| ChiPBench | Wang et al. 2024 | Placement-Routing / End-to-End Chip Placement | 20 circuits from CPU, GPU, and microcontroller domains | End-to-end final PPA rather than only surrogate placement metrics; compares intermediate vs final outcomes | Open benchmark reported by authors; paper states benchmark availability, exact repo/license not stated in retrieved evidence (from paper search results) | Important cautionary benchmark: surrogate placement metrics can misalign with final chip QoR. The same lesson likely applies to PCB generation, where schematic correctness alone may mispredict routability or manufacturability. |
26→
27→
28→*Table: This table summarizes major open datasets, benchmark suites, and evaluation frameworks used in machine-learning and generative EDA through 2025. It highlights scale, metrics, availability, and how each resource does—or does not—support evaluation of an open-hardware control-PCB generation pipeline.*
29→
30→## 2. HDL / RTL Generation Benchmarks
31→
32→### 2.1 VerilogEval
33→
34→**Citation:** Liu, M., Pinckney, N., Khailany, B., & Ren, H. (2023). VerilogEval: Evaluating Large Language Models for Verilog Code Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2309.07544.
35→
36→VerilogEval is a benchmark comprising 156 Verilog code-generation problems sourced from the HDLBits instructional website, ranging from simple combinational circuits to complex finite state machines (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 1-2). It provides two evaluation tracks—VerilogEval-machine (143 validated problems with auto-generated descriptions) and VerilogEval-human (156 problems with manually crafted descriptions) (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4). Functional correctness is evaluated using the pass@k metric, where a problem is considered solved if at least one of *k* sampled LLM outputs passes simulation against a golden reference using the open-source Icarus Verilog simulator (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 3-4, liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 5-7). The benchmark also includes 8,502 problem-code training pairs mined from GitHub Verilog repositories for supervised fine-tuning experiments (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 5-7). The updated VerilogEval v2 extends evaluation to specification-to-RTL tasks, adds in-context learning support, and provides automated failure classification (pinckney2025revisitingverilogevala pages 1-3). The benchmark is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval (liu2023verilogevalevaluatinglarge pages 2-3). **Relevance:** Directly applicable for evaluating generation of controller/FPGA logic modules that may accompany open control PCBs, though it does not assess board-level concerns such as component selection, connectivity, or manufacturability.
37→
38→### 2.2 RTLLM and RTLLM 2.0
39→
40→**Citation:** Lu, Y., Liu, S., Zhang, Q., & Xie, Z. (2024). RTLLM: An Open-Source Benchmark for Design RTL Generation with Large Language Model. *ASP-DAC 2024*, pp. 722–727. Liu, S., Lu, Y., Fang, W., Li, M., & Xie, Z. (2024). OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–9.
41→
42→RTLLM collects 30 common digital designs (11 arithmetic and 19 logic) with three progressive evaluation goals: a syntax goal (verifiable via synthesis into a netlist), a functionality goal (verifiable via testbench simulation), and a design quality goal (evaluating post-synthesis PPA against human-designed references) (lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3, lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 3-4). RTLLM 2.0 expands the suite to 50 hand-crafted designs covering a broader range of complexities and is accompanied by AssertEval (18 designs for assertion generation) and RTLCoder-Data (80K training samples, including a 7K verified high-quality subset) (liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 1-2, liu2024openllmrtlopendataset pages 2-3). Each design provides a natural-language description, testbench, and correct reference RTL. Available at https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM (lu2024rtllmanopensource pages 1-3). **Relevance:** More relevant than VerilogEval for evaluating synthesizable digital peripherals or glue logic that may reside on an open control board, but still misses board-level connectivity, ERC, and component/package constraints.
43→
44→### 2.3 CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems)
45→
46→**Citation:** Pinckney, N. et al. (2025). Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems: A Next-Generation Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating Large Language Models and Agents on RTL Design and Verification. *ArXiv*, abs/2506.14074.
47→
48→CVDP is a next-generation benchmark comprising 783 problems across 13 task categories authored by experienced hardware engineers, covering RTL generation, verification, debugging, specification alignment, and technical Q&A. It supports both non-agentic and agentic task formats, with state-of-the-art models achieving no more than 34% pass@1 on code generation. **Relevance:** Especially valuable for evaluating agentic HDL workflows involving iterative debug and verification loops, analogous to how a PCB design agent would need to iterate on ERC/DRC feedback.
49→
50→### 2.4 VeriContaminated
51→
52→**Citation:** Wang, Z. et al. (2025). VeriContaminated: Assessing LLM-Driven Verilog Coding for Data Contamination. *IEEE ICLAD 2025*, pp. 117–123.
53→
54→VeriContaminated is a meta-evaluation study that analyzes data contamination across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks for state-of-the-art LLMs, finding near-100% contamination rates for some commercial models (wang2025vericontaminatedassessingllmdriven pages 1-2). It employs detection methods including CCD and Min-K% Prob and explores mitigations and their trade-offs for code quality versus fairness. **Relevance:** A critical cautionary resource for any open-hardware generative pipeline—apparent HDL generation success may be inflated by benchmark data leaking into LLM pre-training corpora, undermining reproducibility and trustworthiness of evaluations.
55→
56→### 2.5 TuRTLe
57→
58→**Citation:** Garcia-Gasulla, D. et al. (2025). TuRTLe: A Unified Evaluation of LLMs for RTL Generation. *ArXiv*, abs/2504.01986.
59→
60→TuRTLe is a unified evaluation framework that integrates multiple existing benchmarks (including VerilogEval and RTLLM) and adds synthesis-aware scoring. It evaluates LLMs across cascaded stages—syntax (STX), functional correctness (FNC), and synthesizability (SYN)—using PASS@1, and adds a post-synthesis PPA-score derived from OpenLANE and the open-source SKY130A PDK (garciagasulla2025turtleaunified pages 3-4). **Relevance:** By incorporating synthesis and PPA into the evaluation loop, TuRTLe provides a methodological precedent for PCB benchmarks that should similarly go beyond schematic correctness to assess ERC, DRC, BOM validity, and manufacturability.
61→
62→## 3. Chip-Level Placement and Routing Benchmarks
63→
64→### 3.1 CircuitNet and CircuitNet 2.0
65→
66→**Citation:** Chai, Z. et al. (2022). CircuitNet: An Open-Source Dataset for Machine Learning Applications in Electronic Design Automation. *arXiv:2208.01040*. Jiang, X. et al. (2024). CircuitNet 2.0: An Advanced Dataset for Promoting Machine Learning Innovations in Realistic Chip Design Environment.
67→
68→CircuitNet is the first large-scale open-source dataset for ML in VLSI physical design, built from RISC-V designs mapped to 28nm technology with 10,242 post-flow layouts from 12,960 backend design runs (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 1-2, chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 2-2). It supports three prediction tasks—congestion, DRC violation, and IR drop—evaluated using NRMSE and SSIM for congestion, and ROC-AUC/PR-AUC for DRC and IR drop (chai2022circuitnetanopensource pages 2-2). CircuitNet 2.0 extends coverage to million-gate designs (CPU, GPU, AI chips) at 14nm FinFET and adds timing prediction tasks (jiang2024circuitnet2.0an pages 1-3, jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3). Available at https://circuitnet.github.io/ (jiang2024circuitnet2.0ana pages 1-3). **Relevance:** The strongest open chip-physical-design dataset family; it informs evaluation methodology for congestion/DRC prediction that could be adapted for PCB routing quality assessment, though it does not directly address board-level constraints.
69→
70→### 3.2 ISPD/ICCAD Contest Benchmark Suites
71→
72→**Citation:** Various, 2005–2025. See Kahng, A.B. (2021). Advancing Placement. *ISPD 2021*, pp. 15–22; and individual contest proceedings.
73→
74→The ISPD and ICCAD conferences have hosted 14+ placement and routing contests since 2005, with evolving themes including routability-driven placement (ISPD 2011, DAC 2012), detailed placement (ICCAD 2013), timing-driven placement (ICCAD 2014/2015), and large-scale global routing (ISPD 2025) (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 5-6, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9). Standard metrics include half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL), routing congestion/overflow, timing slack (WNS/TNS), legality, and runtime (kahng2021advancingplacement pages 2-3, jiang2023invitedpaperaccelerating pages 8-9). Benchmarks are typically distributed in Bookshelf or LEF/DEF formats and are publicly available through conference archives (bustany2023the2023mlcad pages 3-4). **Relevance:** Essential methodological precedent for chip placement/routing evaluation; the benchmarking culture and progressive metric design serve as a model for establishing equivalent PCB benchmark contests, though chip-level benchmarks do not capture board-level assembly, EMI, or thermal constraints.
75→
76→### 3.3 EDALearn
77→
78→**Citation:** Pan, J., Chang, C.-C., Xie, Z., Chen, Y., & Li, H.H. (2024). EDALearn: A Comprehensive RTL-to-Signoff EDA Benchmark for Democratized and Reproducible ML for EDA Research. *ICCAD 2024*, pp. 1–8.
79→
80→EDALearn is the first holistic, open-source benchmark suite spanning the complete RTL-to-signoff EDA flow, targeting FreePDK 45nm and ASAP 7nm technology nodes (pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 1-2, pan2024edalearnacomprehensive pages 3-4). It aggregates practical VLSI instances from multiple public sources (IWLS, BOOM/Chipyard, OpenCores, ISCAS, ITC'99) and provides standardized evaluation for routability prediction, IR-drop prediction, and cross-stage analysis with both image-like and vector-based features. **Relevance:** A strong template for how an open PCB benchmark should connect source design intent through intermediate stages to final manufacturable artifacts, supporting reproducibility and cross-technology transfer.

messages.content:
121→**Citation:** D. J. Merrill, "Hungry for fully automated design of embedded systems?" Master's thesis, 2021.
122→
123→**Summary:** This work implements a full automated PCB design pipeline using simulated annealing for placement (with HPWL, congestion, and overlap penalties) and grid-based A* iterative rip-up-and-reroute for routing (20 iterations). The framework is open-source and integrates with KiCad/EAGLE. Laser vias provided approximately 4× speedup over through-hole vias in routing. The SA placer transitioned objective weights from HPWL/congestion to overlap resolution during annealing (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 85-89).
124→
125→**Relevance:** This is the most complete open-source end-to-end PCB automation system in the review and is directly applicable to low-cost open-hardware boards. However, it prioritizes layout-vs-schematic completion over strict DRC cleanliness, meaning post-processing may be needed for manufacturing.
126→
127→---
128→
129→## 6. Learning-Based and Classical Autorouting for PCBs
130→
131→### 6.1 Li et al., "FanoutNet: A Neuralized PCB Fanout Automation Method Using Deep Reinforcement Learning," AAAI 2023
132→
133→**Citation:** H. Li, J. Zhang, N. Xu, and M. Liu, "FanoutNet: A neuralized PCB fanout automation method using deep reinforcement learning," in *Proc. AAAI Conf. AI*, vol. 37, pp. 8554–8561, Jun. 2023. doi:10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030.
134→
135→**Summary:** FanoutNet is the first automated PCB fanout method, using PPO-trained policy and value networks with CNN + attention encoders, combined with a multi-constraint router (MCR) for verification. It achieved 100% routability on all tested open-source and industrial PCB cases, with average wirelength improved by 6.8% over prior methods. Open-source benchmarks: average 25 vias, 1,160 mm wirelength, 50 s runtime; industrial cases: average 270 vias, 245 min runtime. Inference after training was completed in seconds (li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 7-8, li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 6-7, li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2).
136→
137→**Relevance:** FanoutNet directly addresses a critical PCB routing bottleneck (BGA/QFP fanout) that appears in motor controller boards with microcontrollers. The 100% routability achievement is particularly valuable for ensuring manufacturability.
138→
139→### 6.2 Liao et al., "A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach for Global Routing," *J. Mech. Design*, 2020
140→
141→**Citation:** H. Liao, W. Zhang, X. Dong, B. Poczos, K. Shimada, and L. B. Kara, "A deep reinforcement learning approach for global routing," *J. Mech. Design*, vol. 142, no. 6, Nov. 2020. doi:10.1115/1.4045044.
142→
143→**Summary:** This work formulates global routing as a DQN-based RL problem, demonstrating superiority over sequential A* on parameterized grid routing problems. With optimized discount factor (γ = 0.9), the DQN router achieved lower wirelength than A* in 97.5% of 40 test problems, with zero overflow in all cases (versus occasional positive overflow for A*). The work also developed a parameterized routing problem generator for training data creation (liao2020adeepreinforcement pages 9-11, liao2020adeepreinforcement pages 11-13).
144→
145→**Relevance:** The conjoint optimization mechanism (considering downstream nets when routing current nets) is valuable for PCB routing where net interactions are critical. The problem generator concept could be adapted to create PCB-specific training instances. The main limitation is that experiments used abstract grid worlds rather than real PCB geometries.
146→
147→### 6.3 Huang et al., "Ranking Cost: Building An Efficient and Scalable Circuit Routing Planner with Evolution-Based Optimization," arXiv 2021
148→
149→**Citation:** S. Huang, B. Wang, D. Li, J. Hao, T. Chen, and J. Zhu, "Ranking cost: Building an efficient and scalable circuit routing planner with evolution-based optimization," *arXiv:2110.03939*, Oct. 2021. doi:10.48550/arxiv.2110.03939.
150→
151→**Summary:** Ranking Cost uses OpenAI-ES to jointly learn net routing order and cost maps for A*-based sequential routing. On 64×64 grid maps with obstacles (10 net pairs), it achieved 32% success rate with average length 358.5, versus 26% and 367.5 for Seq A*(200). On simpler maps (16×16, 4 pairs), Ranking Cost achieved 100% success versus 96% for exhaustively sampled A*. Runtime was 38.3 s/map, acceptable given the quality gains (huang2021rankingcostbuilding pages 7-9).
152→
153→**Relevance:** The learned net-ordering strategy is directly applicable to PCB autorouting, where routing order significantly affects completion rate. The evolution-based approach avoids the sample-inefficiency issues of policy-gradient RL.
154→
155→---
156→
157→## 7. Benchmarks and Metrics
158→
159→Across the reviewed literature, the following benchmarks and metrics are predominantly used:
160→
161→- **ISPD 2005/2015/2019 benchmarks**: Standard for evaluating VLSI placement and routing quality (HPWL, congestion, routed wirelength, DRC violations) (lin2020dreamplace2.0opensource pages 3-4, liu2021globalplacementwith pages 4-4).
162→- **ICCAD 2004 benchmarks**: Commonly used for mixed-size placement evaluation (lai2023chipformertransferablechip pages 15-17, cheng2023assessmentofreinforcement pages 10-11).
163→- **MacroPlacement GitHub (Cheng et al.)**: Open testcases on modern process nodes (NG45, TSMC 7nm) with full evaluation scripts (cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 1-2, cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 2-3).
164→- **Industrial PCB benchmarks**: Used by FanoutNet (11 open-source + 5 industrial cases) and Murphy (6 boards) (li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 7-8, murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 1-5).
165→- **Primary metrics**: Half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL), routed wirelength, congestion rate, routability/completion rate, DRC violation count, via count, and wall-clock runtime.
166→
167→No widely adopted, standardized PCB placement/routing benchmark suite analogous to ISPD contests exists for PCBs, which remains a significant gap in the field.
168→
169→---
170→
171→## 8. Key Findings and Implications for Open-Hardware PCB Automation
172→
173→**RL-based chip placement methods have shown promise but face practical barriers.** Google's Circuit Training approach, while influential, was shown by independent assessment to underperform strengthened SA baselines on most benchmarks while consuming orders of magnitude more compute (cheng2023assessmentofreinforcement pages 10-11, cheng2023assessmentofreinforcement pages 9-10, cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 12-13). The proxy-cost objective used by CT correlates weakly with final post-route PPA, a critical concern for manufacturable PCB design (cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 12-13).
174→
175→**GPU-accelerated analytical placement (DREAMPlace) offers the most practical foundation.** With ~14× speedup and matching wirelength quality, DREAMPlace's open-source, differentiable framework is extensible to PCB-specific objectives (lin2020dreamplace2.0opensource pages 3-4).
176→
177→**PCB-specific methods remain in early stages.** Crocker's RL-based PCB placement was limited to ~12-component ring topologies (crocker2021physicallyconstrainedpcb pages 41-49); Murphy's NN fitness predictor showed promising routability estimation but was not integrated into a full design loop (murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 36-40); and Merrill's SA-based pipeline is the most complete open-source system but lacks DRC guarantees (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 85-89).
178→
179→**FanoutNet represents a practical advance in PCB routing automation**, achieving 100% routability on industrial cases with 6.8% wirelength improvement (li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2).
180→
181→**For low-cost motor/sensor control boards** (typically 50–200 components, 2–4 layers), the most practical near-term approach would combine: (1) SA or DREAMPlace-derived placement with PCB-specific constraints, (2) Murphy-style NN routability prediction as a fitness function, (3) A*-based rip-up-and-reroute or FanoutNet-style RL fanout for routing, and (4) explicit DRC checking in the optimization loop rather than relying on proxy costs alone. The absence of standardized PCB benchmarks remains a critical gap that limits reproducible comparison of methods.
182→
